10.1080_00045600903378960: We examine how access regimes—defined as a set of interrelated institutions and organizations—facilitated the flow of production resources and benefits to drive patterns of land change in the Brazilian Cerrado. Adopting a mesoscale analytical framework grounded in new institutional economics (NIE) and the theory of access, we move beyond a narrow focus on property rights to include land-leasing and production contracts, as well as the role of cooperatives and firms as distinct organizational actors. We analyzed remotely sensed data (Landsat MSS, TM, and ETM) for eastern Mato Grosso state across three time periods: 1972/1973–1986, 1986–1992, and 1992–2002. These geospatial data were integrated with qualitative and archival data to evaluate how land managers accessed natural and productive resources, such as credit, technology, and inputs. By comparing lands under private colonization schemes with those under no such schemes, we identified spatial and temporal variability in Cerrado conversion and land-use intensification. This approach allows us to connect macroeconomic drivers to place-based socioeconomic factors, demonstrating how specific institutional and organizational synergies influence land-use decisions and regional land-cover outcomes.
10.1080_00045600903550295: We adopted an integrated research approach to evaluate the social-ecological impacts of concurrent reservoir inundation from the Three Gorges Project (TGP) and national reforestation policies on farmland supply, carrying capacity, resettlement, and alternative employment. Focusing on a case study of Baota and Dongcun townships in Yunyang County, Chongqing, we conducted an in-depth assessment of every farmland patch across a study area of 80.9 $\text{km}^2$. Our methodology combined quantitative spatial analysis with qualitative social data; specifically, we utilized high-resolution QuickBird satellite imagery, thematic maps, and geographic information system (GIS) techniques to generate digital maps of land use and farmland quality. To capture the human dimension of these land-cover changes, we integrated field assessments, interviews with officials, and questionnaire surveys targeting local villagers. By synthesizing these data sources, we were able to compute farmland quality ratings and model the combined effects of "development" (reservoir construction) and "conservation" (defarming) on rural communities. This comprehensive package of methods allowed us to analyze the interaction between the loss of fertile riverside lands and the mandated reforestation of steep-slope farmlands within the specific geographical time-space regime of the Three Gorges region.
10.1080_00045608.2010.532741: We investigate the social and economic transformations altering the function of the household in rural Sri Lanka by examining domestic and international migration as constitutive elements of place. Our research is situated in two contrasting rural sites: Kumburegama South in the Galle District of the south, and Alut Henegama in the Kurunegala District on the margins of the Dry Zone. To capture the complexities of household-migration interactions, we employed a mixed-methods approach consisting of a survey questionnaire administered to one hundred households, complemented by semi-structured interviews with a subsample of fifty migrants or their family members. Data collection took place in 2007, focusing on key variables such as household size, land access, mean income, and remittance dependency. We specifically contrast the drivers and outcomes of domestic migration—primarily young, unmarried individuals moving to garment factories in Colombo and Galle—with international migration, largely comprising married women working as housekeepers in the Middle East. By looking inside the household, we apply a lens of cooperation and conflict to analyze how these different migration streams reshape bargaining power, status, and livelihood strategies within the family unit.
10.1080_00045608.2011.567936: We develop an integrated research approach to model the spatially detailed carbon balance between consumption-based emissions and vegetation carbon sequestration. Using Florida as a case study for the year 2001, we estimate household carbon emissions at the U.S. Census tract scale, focusing on the energy and transportation sectors. Residential energy emissions are calculated by combining Census 2000 household size data with average consumption levels from the U.S. Energy Information Administration, while transportation emissions are derived from National Household Travel Survey transferability data and U.S. EPA emission coefficients. To quantify carbon sinks, we employ biophysical remote sensing techniques to estimate gross primary production (GPP) at a 1-km resolution, utilizing the 2001 National Land Cover Database, AVHRR normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI), and solar radiation data. These GPP estimates are adjusted for plant respiration and aggregated to the Census tract scale. Finally, we utilize univariate analysis of variance (ANOVA) to examine the relationship between carbon balance—the difference between household emissions and vegetation assimilation—and four settlement density categories: urban, suburban, exurban, and rural.
10.1080_00045608.2011.569659: We examine energy poverty in the rural United States by conceptualizing it as a geographical assemblage of networked materialities and socioeconomic relations. To illustrate this approach, we focus on the rural landscapes of Greene, Lenoir, and Wayne counties in eastern North Carolina, a region characterized by high poverty rates and a declining economic base. Our methodology integrates quantitative socioeconomic data from the American Community Survey and the recipient database of the Wayne Action Group for Economic Solvency (WAGES, Inc.) with qualitative ethnographic insights. Specifically, we employed a typical case sampling technique to select participants from the WAGES weatherization database, conducting in-depth interviews with seventeen households receiving weatherization assistance during the summer of 2009. By analyzing these interviews alongside data on energy provision infrastructures and the material conditions of the home, we trace the intersections of individual biographies—including health crises and economic precariousness—with the techno-social networks of energy distribution. This multi-scalar approach allows us to highlight the lived realities of energy poverty and the specific networked configurations that produce and sustain it in a rural context.
10.1080_00045608.2011.584289: Using a case study of the Perote Valley in central Veracruz, Mexico, we employ an integrated social, environmental, and geospatial approach to analyze how urban-driven demand for construction aggregates affects rural agricultural lands and livelihoods. To examine land-cover changes associated with aggregate surface mining between 1995 and 2006, we compared aerial photographs, satellite imagery, and Global Positioning System (GPS) field maps of the valley. This geospatial analysis allowed us to quantify changes in mine surface area and identify spatial patterns of extraction relative to accessibility. To complement these data, we conducted sixty-six formal household interviews across three agrarian communities to investigate the socioeconomic drivers of land-use conversion. Key variables analyzed include the price of aggregates, agricultural returns, and the rate of land recultivation following extraction. By synthesizing these data, we evaluate the transition from agricultural to aggregate-based land use and the resulting impact on the long-term agricultural potential of the region.
10.1080_00045608.2011.596392: Drawing on a decade of qualitative fieldwork, I employ an actor-oriented livelihoods approach to examine how Hmong ethnic minority households in the Sa Pa district of L`ao Cai province, Vietnam, negotiate the tensions between economic liberalization and authoritarian socialist political structures. To understand the nuanced decision-making processes underlying rural livelihoods, I utilize the notion of the "social interface" as a heuristic device to analyze the points of linkage and confrontation where discrepancies in power, knowledge, and cultural interpretation are mediated. This framework is further extended by integrating concepts of everyday politics and covert resistance—specifically the use of "hidden transcripts"—to reveal how actors may shirk or sidestep market integration. The study focuses on three primary diversification strategies—cardamom cultivation, textile trade, and tourism trekking—that supplement otherwise subsistence-based livelihoods. By weaving together ethnographic data with an analysis of state policy, the actions of local officials, and ethnically embedded social relations, I investigate how Hmong individuals navigate new economic opportunities while resisting unwanted market dependence, thereby highlighting the micro-foundations of macro-processes in the northern Vietnam uplands.
10.1080_00045608.2011.620501: We employ a mixed-methods approach that fuses the conceptual frameworks of political ecology with the methodological rigor of land change science to examine "contentious land change" (C-LC) in the Amazon's arc of deforestation. Our study focuses on southeastern Pará, Brazil, a region characterized by a long history of antagonism between wealthy largeholders and the rural poor. To describe the social processes expediting forest loss, we combine a detailed case study of land conflict over a specific largeholding with a regional-scale statistical model. By integrating the theory of contentious politics with structural political ecology, we analyze how the interaction of diverse and adversarial agents—including ranchers, landless farmers, and social movement organizations—drives deforestation. This methodology allows us to test whether land cover change is the result of independent microeconomic optimization by isolated land managers or is instead enhanced by social inequalities and competing land claims. Through the use of unique geographic and historical data and regression analyses, we evaluate the impact of Direct Action Land Reform (DALR) and agrarian conflict on environmental degradation across the study area.
10.1080_00045608.2011.641861: We employ a multimethod, semi-reflexive approach to examine the technical and political dimensions of a six-year United Nations (UN) development initiative in the Brazilian Amazon. Our study focuses on the intersection of environmental governance and peasant livelihoods, specifically targeting artisanal and small-scale gold mining (*garimpeiros*) communities. To bridge the gap between legal policy abstractions and material geography, we utilized GIS and Shuttle Radar imagery to map contested areas and natural preserves as defined by the 2002 and 2006 amendments to the Brazilian Forest Code. This technical mapping was integrated with multistakeholder consultation processes involving development planners, government ministries, NGO activists, and rural miners to explore the political implications of "crime mapping" and surveillance. By combining high-resolution geospatial analysis with a political ecology framework, we analyze the risks and limitations of UN technical interventions. We specifically investigate how the translation of GIS knowledge can either empower rural communities through "grassroots GIS" or undermine local trust when deployed primarily as an enforcement tool. Through this interdisciplinary lens, we evaluate the tensions between the UN's mandate for environmental protection and the socio-economic realities of informal mining in ecologically sensitive zones.
10.1080_00045608.2011.641897: Drawing from the framework of critical legal geography, I examine the contested political geography of potable water access in the *colonias* of South Texas. I argue that the trajectory of inadequate water access in these predominantly Mexican-American rural and periurban communities was determined by water politics and territorial exclusion rather than solely by poverty or public policy failure. To investigate this, I analyze two companion legal cases from 1971 (*Jimenez v. Hidalgo WCID 2 et al.* and *Fonseca v. Hidalgo WCID 2 et al.*) that challenged the ability of farmer-controlled water control and improvement districts (WCIDs) to unilaterally exclude *colonias* from their territories. My methodology involves a detailed analysis of legislative archives, court records, and the Mexican American Legal Defense and Education Fund (MALDEF) archives. Rather than focusing exclusively on final rulings, I trace the evolution of legal reasoning and shifting discursive tactics throughout the litigation process. By interrogating these legal texts, I explore how the debate shifted from political voting rights to narratives of market forces and service provision, effectively transforming *colonias* residents from political actors with governance standing into passive consumers of dysfunctional public services.
10.1080_00045608.2011.652871:
We employ a regional ethnography to examine the intersection of neoliberal socioeconomic change and transnational migration in the Totonacapan region of Veracruz, Mexico. Our approach focuses on how macro-level processes of neoliberal restructuring are filtered through local historical trajectories and cultural understandings to produce varied landscapes of economic subjectivity. We conducted three months of ethnographic fieldwork in 2007 across four municipios: Papantla and Tecolutla in the predominantly Mestizo coastal plain, and Cuahuitlán and Filomeno Mata in the more indigenous highlands of the Sierra Madre Oriental. 

Our data were derived from fifty-three semistructured interviews with state and regional officials, migrants, their families, farmers, and local leaders across sixteen small communities. These were supplemented by seven half-day focus group sessions with men, women, youth, and community leaders to trace agrarian histories and migration patterns, as well as collaborative projects with local youth. We synthesized these observations and experiences through a qualitative analysis of interview and focus group transcripts, utilizing content analysis to identify recurring themes and compiling community-specific histories of government programs and major crops. Through this immersive method, we analyze how differing conceptions of social responsibility and ethnic identity influence whether communities experience migration as a source of severe dislocation or as a managed process for the collective benefit.
10.1080_00045608.2011.653732: We employ a critical theoretical approach to disclose the elements of Nazi spatial theory, specifically examining how the "reactionary modernism" of the Third Reich manifested through the interrelated processes of deterritorialization and reterritorialization. To delineate this "dark geography," we focus our analysis on the contributions of two key figures: the legal and political theorist Carl Schmitt and the geographer Walter Christaller. We analyze Schmitt’s conceptualization of *Großraum* (greater space) as the politico-judicial justification for the geographical expansion of the Reich and the creation of a new international spatial order. Simultaneously, we examine Christaller’s application of central place theory to the planning of "empty space" in conquered Eastern territories, particularly Poland, following the forced removal of non-Germanized populations. By synthesizing these theoretical contributions within the broader context of Nazi biopolitics and the pursuit of *Lebensraum*, we argue that the regime utilized a combination of instrumental rationality and antimodernist romanticism to impose a violent new relationship between people and space. Our analysis situates these figures alongside the established geopolitical work of Karl Haushofer to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the spatial strategies used to construct the Nazi empire.
10.1080_00045608.2012.671127: Drawing on a nature-society geography approach to health and well-being, I examine the public health crisis of chronic arsenic poisoning in rural Bangladesh. To expound upon the multifaceted nature of health within the context of development, I analyze the socioecological parameters, economic factors, and political drivers that have contributed to the mass poisoning of millions of people through the consumption of arsenic-laced groundwater. My approach integrates insights from political ecology and feminist geographies of well-being to investigate the complex intersections of environmental change, development interventions—specifically the large-scale promotion of tubewells—and embodied health. I pay particular attention to the production of "contaminated citizens," focusing on the interlinked ways that water contamination influences not only physical illness and morbidity but also social stigma, ostracization, and emotional distress. By analyzing the lived experiences of those affected, I highlight how the trajectories of health and well-being are shaped by the contradictions of development endeavors and the uneven social access to safe water alternatives.
10.1080_00045608.2012.671129: We present a case study demonstrating the simultaneous application of social network and spatial analytical methods to model the transmission of cholera and shigellosis in rural Bangladesh. Utilizing a spatially referenced longitudinal demographic database of approximately 200,000 people in Matlab from 1983 to 2003, we analyzed disease incidence patterns across both geographic space and kinship-based social networks. To operationalize these relationships, we constructed distance matrices to model spatial proximity and kinship-based matrices using a complete network design, where nodes represent *baris* (household clusters) and edges are defined by individual-level migrations. We first calculated Moran's I statistics to measure clustering within the social and spatial matrices independently. To further isolate the influence of social ties from the local neighborhood context, we developed combined spatial effects and spatial disturbance models. These models allowed us to analyze simultaneous spatial and social effects while controlling for known covariates and unknown underlying spatial autocorrelation. Through this integrated approach, we sought to determine whether person-to-person transmission via kinship networks or environmental transmission via geographic proximity served as the primary driver for each bacterial diarrheal disease.
10.1080_00045608.2012.671133: Drawing on long-term research initiated in 2000, I employ a mixed-methods approach to analyze health discourses and the use of traditional medicine in rural northeast South Africa. The study focuses on the Mzinti community in Mpumalanga Province, a region characterized by significant political and spatial transitions following the 1994 democratic elections. To generate a contextually rich understanding of health decision-making, I integrate quantitative data from a structured survey of 478 randomly selected households—which captured demographic characteristics, livelihood patterns, and the collection of medicinal plants—with qualitative data derived from fifty semistructured interviews with male and female household heads. These interviews specifically explored perceptions of medical options and the use of *sangomas*, while participant observation and additional interviews with health care providers and government agency representatives provided further depth. By comparing the discrepancies between survey reportage and interview narratives, I work to uncover "subaltern health narratives" and examine how sociocultural systems, historical geographies, and conflicting regulatory frameworks mediate the intersection of traditional and biomedical health practices.
10.1080_00045608.2012.678040: We employ a three-step methodology to analyze the factors and trends shaping cattle ranching development and its implications for deforestation in the Brazilian Legal Amazon. First, we formulate an integrated analytical framework based on a systematic literature review and qualitative data from semistructured interviews conducted with landholders and industry managers across several states, including Pará, Acre, Roraima, and Amazonas. This framework links broad policy and market drivers to specific production dynamics and subsequent land-use outcomes. Second, we utilize secondary statistical data to identify primary trends in production and trade. This includes data from FAOSTAT and the Brazilian ministries of Industry and Trade (MDIC) and Agriculture (MAPA), as well as Federal Inspection Service (SIF) records to track investments in processing facilities. We further analyze municipal-level data from the 1995-1996 and 2006 IBGE agricultural censuses to evaluate changes in herd densities, production systems, and land tenure. Finally, we link these socioeconomic trends with observed deforestation patterns by comparing census data with remote sensing analysis provided by the National Institute of Spatial Research (INPE). This integrated approach allows us to disentangle the differentiated effects of market integration and policy shifts on cattle production and landscape change across the region.
10.1080_00045608.2012.707591: We present a multilevel modeling framework, the FUTure Urban-Regional Environment Simulation (FUTURES), designed to simulate the emergence of landscape spatial structure in urbanizing regions by integrating field-based and object-based representations of land change. Our approach couples three interacting submodels to integrate nonstationary drivers: POTENTIAL, which utilizes multilevel logistic regression to quantify development suitability based on socioeconomic, infrastructural, and environmental factors; DEMAND, which estimates subregional per capita land consumption rates; and a stochastic Patch-Growing Algorithm (PGA). The PGA bridges cell- and object-based representations by using a region-growing mechanism to aggregate cell-level transitions into discrete spatial objects, with parameters empirically estimated to control patch size, shape, and dispersion. We applied this framework to the metropolitan region of Charlotte, North Carolina, simulating land development dynamics from 1996 to 2030. To evaluate the model, we assessed spatial variation in outcomes along an urban-rural continuum using cell- and patch-based metrics of correctness and error. Through simulation experiments, we examined how variations in per capita land consumption and development distribution parameters influence the emergent spatial structure of forests and farmlands.
10.1080_00045608.2012.720228: Drawing upon an anti-essentialist reading of Foucault’s theories on power and governmentality, I employ a theoretical framework merged with concepts from grounded theory, infrastructure studies, and boundary objects to examine the power dynamics inherent in cadastral mapping. Rather than focusing on the map as a static artifact, I invert the analysis to focus on the *use* of maps, decomposing cadastral mapping activities into interactions, boundary objects, and contextual factors to establish how empowerment and limitation occur. This approach is applied to fieldwork conducted in Poland between 2006 and 2008, involving 120 to 138 interviews per year across three distinct sites. The study area is characterized by significant discrepancies—up to 40 percent in rural areas—between officially mapped ownership and actual property usage following the 1989 collapse of socialist governance. By analyzing these discrepancies across multiple scales, I explore the interrelated economic, strategic, legal, and technical dimensions of property rights and the state's construction of calculable territory. Furthermore, the methodology accounts for the contemporary influence of geographic information systems (GIS) and the regulatory controls of Common Agricultural Policy disbursements to analyze competing meanings of accuracy and the limits of the cadastre's calculative power.
10.1080_00045608.2012.720234: We employ a livelihood approach, drawing on geography and related fields, to assess the impacts of argan oil trade on rural poverty in southwestern Morocco. By utilizing this framework, we aim to integrate the study of a biological resource-based niche commodity (BNC) into the complex web of activities through which rural households earn a living, thereby contextualizing the commodity chain. Our methodology involves testing the claim that the commercialization of argan oil—a high-value product endemic to the region—serves as an effective tool for poverty alleviation. We analyze the contribution of argan oil production to household budgets and asset accumulation, comparing its influence against other key variables such as nonfarm work and remittances. This approach allows us to evaluate how agro-ecological conditions, resource scarcity, ownership regimes, and value chain characteristics influence the actual socio-economic outcomes for producers in the Souss region.
10.1080_00045608.2013.756257: We conducted this study in six villages in Matlab, Bangladesh, focusing on a population of approximately 12,000 residents to examine the effects of the local population-environment context on tubewell water quality and childhood diarrheal disease. Between May 2008 and November 2009, we collected monthly duplicate 100-mL water samples from 100 shallow tubewells (< 43 m) to measure culturable *Escherichia coli* concentrations using the most probable number (MPN) Colilert test. Simultaneously, we utilized a longitudinal Health and Demographic Surveillance System to track monthly diarrheal events in children under five. To analyze the spatial relationship between infrastructure and contamination, we developed a detailed geographic database using submeter accuracy GPS surveys to map all households, ponds, and latrines within 200 m of the monitored wells. Latrines were categorized as sanitary or unsanitary based on effluent leakage. We constructed sanitation metrics using circular windows (10–100 m) to measure latrine and population counts, alongside tubewell characteristics such as age, depth, and the presence of a concrete platform. Statistical analyses included Pearson correlation coefficients and multiple regression models, with model selection guided by Akaike's information criterion (AIC), while a logistic regression model was employed to evaluate the relationship between *E. coli* concentrations and childhood diarrhea incidence.
10.1080_00045608.2013.779549: Drawing from geographic engagements with theories of racialization and NIMBYism, I investigate the connections between economic development and the relational construction of racial identities in Madras, Oregon. My analysis focuses on the discourses of local white leadership surrounding two interconnected economic agendas designed to remake the town into an upscale, white community: the entrepreneurial recruitment of a state correctional facility and an urban renewal project emphasizing high-income residential construction and the removal of "blighted" housing. By examining these developments within the specific racial geometry of Jefferson County—a region characterized by entrenched poverty and a diverse population of white, Latino, and Native American residents—I interrogate how "yes in my backyard" (YIMBY) prison politics are galvanized to maintain geographies of racialized privilege. Through this empirical focus, I employ a racial economy perspective to demonstrate how white officials utilized a normative racial framework to reaffirm their own privileged status while stigmatizing marginalized groups. Ultimately, I argue that these redevelopment strategies simultaneously institutionalize carceral expansion and reproduce unequal spaces, illustrating the centrality of race-class formations in shaping local geographies of opportunity and disadvantage.
10.1080_00045608.2014.892338: To examine how the level of detail in market representation influences projected land-use patterns, we employed an abstract, agent-based model (ABM) titled Land Use in an eXurban Environment (LUXE). Designed to generalize across existing spatially explicit models of the North American urban-rural fringe, LUXE utilizes a simplified environment of homogeneous land parcels where price variation is driven by locational characteristics—specifically proximity to the urban center and local open-space amenities—as well as the heterogeneous preferences and budgets of agents. We conducted a series of experiments incorporating agents representing land buyers and sellers to scrutinize the effects of specific market-based decision-making behaviors. Specifically, we tested the impacts of incorporating budget constraints and competitive bidding mechanisms against simpler utility maximization algorithms. By varying the complexity of these market interactions, we analyzed the resulting quantity of land-use change and the degree of sprawl using descriptive statistics, linear regression, and visualization. This experimental approach allowed us to isolate how the inclusion of endogenous land price mechanisms and financial limitations alters the simulated landscapes compared to models that neglect these market structures.
10.1080_00045608.2014.944453: Drawing on a synthesis of Marxist theories of primitive accumulation and Foucault’s conceptualization of governmentality, I explore the relationship between the techniques of colonial administration and the stability of rule in colonial Zambia (Northern Rhodesia). I specifically examine the transition in the 1920s from the coercive charter rule of the British South Africa Company to the administration of the Colonial Office, a period marked by the cessation of widespread rural unrest and the expansion of an extractive economy. Utilizing archival and secondary sources, I analyze two primary spatial and political interventions: the implementation of indirect rule through Native authorities and the constitution of Native reserves. I argue that these mechanisms functioned as a new mode of governing that sought to align the political landscape with the requirements of extractive capitalism by producing new economic subjectivities. By weaving together political economy and poststructural notions of power, I examine how these techniques worked to stabilize the proletarianization and dispossession of African populations, ultimately resolving the central contradictions of colonial rule—the tension between promoting radical social transformation and maintaining political order—across the sub-Saharan African colonial context.
10.1080_00045608.2014.985626: We employ a case study approach to examine mobilizations for agro-ecological alternatives and food sovereignty in the Biobío region of southern Chile, focusing specifically on a network of peasant women known as *curadoras de semillas* (seed curators). Our analysis is grounded in a combination of ethnographic and collaborative methods conducted between 2010 and 2013. The primary data consists of twenty-three in-depth interviews—eight with movement leaders and fifteen with *curadoras*—obtained through a snowball sampling approach. These interviews, which averaged two hours in length and were mostly conducted in the participants' homes, were recorded, transcribed, and coded using NVivo 10 software. To supplement these interviews, we drew upon participant observation at seed exchange events and a long-standing collaboration with Chile's National Association of Rural and Indigenous Women (ANAMURI). We frame our analysis by integrating three theoretical perspectives: food sovereignty as an analytical and activist principle, postdevelopment and alter-globalization approaches to capitalist alternatives, and literature on the spatial politics of local mobilizations. Through this methodology, we explore how these women utilize horizontal networks and traditional knowledge to challenge the hegemonic agro-export regime and its universalizing spatiality.
10.1080_00045608.2015.1060921:
We employed a mixed-methods approach, combining quantitative analysis of household surveys with qualitative interviews and participant observations, to investigate the cross-scale processes shaping rural land-use decisions in the Poyang Lake Region (PLR) of China. To account for locational and environmental variability, we stratified villages based on adjusted elevation (high vs. low risk of flooding) and proximity to county capitals (within vs. beyond 5 km), randomly selecting eight villages across these four categories. Household surveys were conducted in January 2007, targeting approximately 25% of households per village. 

To analyze the data, we developed a three-level regression model for the region's two dominant crops—one-season rice and cotton—to capture the nested effects of plot-level biophysical properties (Level 1), household characteristics (Level 2), and village location (Level 3). This quantitative framework allowed us to evaluate the influence of urban job markets, represented by off-farm income, on individual plot crop choices. We supplemented these findings with qualitative data from interviews and field observations to account for macrolevel forces—such as national policy reforms affecting agricultural markets and regional government interventions—that were not fully captured by the surveys. Together, these methods enabled us to examine how macrolevel forces interact with microlevel biophysical, demographic, and locational factors to drive household land-use decisions.
10.1080_00045608.2015.1113110: We investigated the space-time paths of twenty-seven widowed women in the village of Nakagongo, south central Uganda, to examine the relationship between mobility and livelihood opportunities. Adopting a methodological pluralism that integrates quantitative and qualitative data, we employed GPS tracking to accurately map the subjects' activity spaces and daily routines over a three-month period, avoiding the distortions common in self-reported time diaries. These data were triangulated with participant observation conducted during three two-week intervals over one year, as well as two-hour semistructured interviews with each participant. Our analysis utilized ArcGIS to construct space-time paths and time prisms, allowing us to evaluate the spatial budget and time use of women with varying levels of livelihood diversification. We specifically examined variables such as age, education, family capital, and health, alongside the frequency of use of various transportation modes—including walking, bicycle taxis, motorcycle taxis, buses, and cars. Finally, we applied statistical regressions to determine the effect of these transport modes on livelihood, treating time as a critical resource for pursuing income-generating activities.
10.1080_00167428.2020.1779592: Drawing from the burgeoning field of black geographies, I conducted a long-term ethnographic study between 2014 and 2018 to examine how the *boucherie*—the ritualized butchering of a hog—serves as a mechanism for black placemaking among rural Creole communities in southwestern Louisiana. My methodological approach integrated dozens of semistructured interviews with 29 participants, which were transcribed verbatim and coded using NVivo software, alongside dozens of hours of participant observation. To provide a fulsome portrait of this community and the socio-economic context of the study area—specifically within Acadia, St. Landry, Lafayette, and St. Martin parishes—I supplemented my primary fieldwork with archival research, including the analysis of census data, government reports, community-based magazines, and newspaper articles. By weaving together these qualitative and archival sources, I analyze the *boucherie* not merely as a livelihood-maintenance tactic, but as a "memory performance" and an embodied practice. This approach allows me to demonstrate how the ritual spatializes Creole cultural identity, transforming rural landscapes into symbolic sites of endurance, kinship, and resistance.
10.1080_00167428.2020.1786384: We utilized the Nebraska Roots Migration Survey, an online instrument adapted from a previous study in Vermont, to examine the residential decision-making and place attachments of individuals who spent at least part of their adolescence in Nebraska. Distributed via social media networks in February and March 2019, the survey gathered 1,827 fully completed responses representing all 93 counties in the state. We collected demographic data, multiple-choice factors for staying, leaving, or returning, and open-ended narratives. To facilitate between-group comparisons, we adopted the U.S. Census definition of rurality, classifying communities with fewer than 2,500 residents as rural and those with 2,500 or more as urban. We specifically categorized "stayers" as those who had never lived outside Nebraska for a full year or more (excluding schooling or military service), further distinguishing between "rural hometowners," "rural residents," and "all-rural stayers." Quantitative data were analyzed using SPSS via cross-tabulation and chi-square tests ($p \le 0.05$). Qualitative narratives were then coded thematically using Dedoose, employing a biographical approach to provide nuanced context to the statistical findings and explore the complex emotional and social drivers of immobility.
10.1080_00167428.2020.1852406: We use the Mongolian Kazakh population as a case study to examine how strong place attachments evolve into place-based narratives of home and identity for diasporic communities. Drawing upon a conceptual framework that integrates the literatures of nostalgia, place attachment, and the rural idyll, we specifically employ Scannell and Gifford’s Person-Process-Place (PPP) model to analyze the multidimensional nature of these bonds. Our approach focuses on recent urban in-migrants in Ulaanbaatar whose ethnic and cultural histories are tied to the remote, rural region of western Mongolia. We explore the permutations of place identity as expressed through daily life, investigating how these attachments are communicated to younger generations—who may have limited personal experience with the rural homeland—via stories, material artifacts, and community celebrations. By analyzing the interplay between memory, imagination, and mobility, we seek to demonstrate how former residents of western Mongolia (re)create a "rural idyll" that serves as a social symbol of Kazakh culture and history, thereby maintaining collective place valuing despite residence in a globalized urban environment.
10.1080_00167428.2020.1855583:
We utilize geospatial data for 816,000 parcels provided by the Wisconsin State Cartographer's Office (WSCO) to analyze the extent and value of non-resident property ownership in Wisconsin's Northwoods. Our study area encompasses twenty-one counties in the northern third of the state, covering approximately 21,000 square miles. Using Version 3 data from the WSCO parcel-fabric database—which includes attributes such as owner mailing addresses, assessed fair-market values, and property tax totals—we employ the parcel tax mailing address as a proxy for primary or secondary property status. To process this data, we used Excel functions and the column-wizard tool to separate address fields, followed by geocoding via ESRI's Business Analyst Extension. 

Our methodological approach is twofold. First, we use ArcMap GIS geoprocessing tools (clip, spatial join) to identify regional patterns of non-resident land ownership and calculate summary statistics for parcel size and assessed value. Second, we conduct a localized economic analysis of four specific school districts—Grantsburg, Hayward, Hurley, and Northland Pines—to determine the impact of non-resident property taxes on public-school funding. By categorizing owners into local, non-local Wisconsin residents, and out-of-state owners, we examine how varying concentrations of tourism amenities influence capital accumulation and local institutional sustainability.
10.1080_00167428.2020.1865816:
To examine the emergence of distinct forms of citizenship in rural commodity frontiers, I employ a qualitative, multi-disciplinary approach centered on the salmon industry in the Los Lagos region of Chile. Drawing on six years of fieldwork (2012–2018) conducted by a team of geographers, anthropologists, and sociologists, the study utilizes data collected across at least eight districts. The methodological framework integrates ethnographic research—including six-month immersions in the localities of Calbuco and Cochamó—nearly 70 semi-structured interviews, and four participatory mapping experiences in Cochamó, Quellón, Quemchi, and Curaco de Velez, with workshops ranging from four to ten sessions each. 

I analyzed the resulting data using Atlas.ti software, coding transcripts through analytical categories derived from resource frontier literature and the concept of rural citizenship. This approach allows for the exploration of the trialectic relationship between place identity, commodity production, and democratic institutions. By focusing on the spatial patterns, monetary flows, and state-subject interactions within this specific regional context, I identify how the neoliberalization of nature and the transformation of the landscape into a salmon-producing territory shape the political participation and identity performance of rural inhabitants.
10.1080_00167428.2020.1871298: To construct alternative scenarios for rural Croatia in 2030, we integrated quantitative and qualitative approaches, acknowledging the country's postsocialist transition and EU accession as the primary framework. Our methodological design combined factor and cluster analysis to establish a rural typology, alongside Monte Carlo simulations to model potential outcomes. To incorporate expert knowledge and refine these projections, we employed the Delphi method, conducting two rounds of written questionnaires with 37 rural experts. This mixed-methods approach allowed us to synthesize complex socioeconomic variables—including demographic decline, unemployment levels, and the impacts of the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP)—across the Croatian countryside. By weaving together these statistical tools and expert consensus, we developed four distinct scenarios: Rural Renaissance, Shift, Road to Nowhere, and Growth without Development. These scenarios serve as documented assumptions to explore possible future paths for the Croatian rural landscape and to facilitate a broader discussion on neoendogenous rural development paradigms.
10.1080_00167428.2021.1890996: To document the apparent loss of sense of local heritage among inhabitants of the Belgian countryside, we employed a mixed-methods approach designed to support the reflections of the NGO 'Qualité Village Wallonie' (QVW). We first conducted seven semi-structured interviews and workshops with QVW staff and administrators, leveraging their multi-decade experience to trace longitudinal changes in community interactions with local heritage. These qualitative insights were then used in conjunction with an e-survey administered via 'framaforms' between March and June 2020. The survey, which garnered 1,091 responses from across Wallonia, utilized a short, playful questionnaire to encourage participation and minimize abandonment. We collected socio-demographic data—including age, gender, housing ownership, and duration of residence—and employed 18 Likert-scale items to measure perceived heritage richness, personal involvement, and the perceived contribution of heritage to local life. Participants were also asked to identify emblematic local places and heritage elements. This methodological framework allowed us to position individuals within a conceptual triangular model of heritage—defined by the dimensions of attachment, resource, and charge—to analyze how the perception of rural heritage is evolving in a globalized, suburbanized context.
10.1080_00167428.2021.1897812: To examine the emergence of a contemporary mountain imaginary in the Galician Highlands, we employed a qualitative research design centered on the Trevinca Massif. Drawing upon Debarbieux’s conceptualization of social imaginaries of space and existing theorizations of mountains as socially framed spatial categories and national discourses, we analyzed recent local narratives to determine whether they reflect the construction of a national landscape or are driven by global processes. Our empirical approach consisted of conducting semistructured interviews with local actors, the resulting data from which were processed through analytical coding. We then utilized semiotic clustering to derive three distinct narratives. By contrasting these results with the broader evolution of Western mountain imaginaries and the historical genealogy of Galician national landscape imaginaries—which have traditionally privileged coastal and agricultural motifs—we sought to explain the socio-spatial emergence of the "Galician Highlands" as a category. This methodology allowed us to evaluate the influence of global forces, specifically tourism and promotion, and the role of disputed "natural borders" in shaping the current perception of the region.
10.1080_00167428.2021.1906669:
We employ a life-stage approach to decompose spatiotemporal population changes along an urban-rural gradient in continental Spain from 2002 to 2017. Utilizing longitudinal demographic data from the Spanish Continuous Municipal Register (INE), we analyze population dynamics at a municipal scale across 7,914 municipalities. To capture the nuances of the urban-rural gradient, we classify municipalities into seven distinct classes based on a combination of demographic size—categorized as urban (>30,000 inhabitants), large, medium, or small—and their GIS-calculated distance from urban centers, distinguishing between suburban (<20 km) and rural (>20 km) areas.

Our analysis focuses on the absolute population and three specific birth cohorts defined by their life-course stages during the study period: a young, working-age cohort (born 1973–1992), a mature working-age cohort (born 1953–1972), and a retirement cohort (born 1943–1952). We calculate the number of inhabitants and the percentage of population variation for each group across the seven municipality classes. Furthermore, we establish population change typologies by analyzing the number and percentage of municipalities experiencing increases or decreases in both absolute population and within each of the three study cohorts.
10.1080_00167428.2021.1925897:
We employ a multi-case study approach to explore young people's visions for life in the countryside across four independent research sites in Latin America: north Argentina, east Bolivia, south Ecuador, and south Mexico. Our methodology is grounded in the principles of social constructivism and reflexivity, treating research as a joint process of knowledge production. Given the diversity of local contexts—ranging from tropical rainforests and neotropical forests to semideciduous dry forests and transitional Puna-Yungas zones—we avoided a fixed category of "youth" and instead applied flexible, participative methods adapted to each site. 

Between 2015 and 2019, we collected data using a variety of qualitative and quantitative tools, including participatory workshops involving drawing and acting, semi-structured and ethnographic "go-along" interviews, non-participant observation, and structured surveys. Participants included secondary and university students, as well as young adults and indigenous peasants. Data analysis involved triangulation of visual and textual outputs, utilizing Grounded Theory, framework analysis, and descriptive statistics via software such as atlas.ti and IBM SPSS. By analyzing similarities and complementarities across these case studies, we identify crosscutting themes regarding (im)mobilities, place-based belonging, and the motivations that drive young people to either stay in or leave their rural communities.
10.1080_00330120903543756: To assess the potential risk to humans from wildfires within the U.S.-Mexico border zone of San Diego County, California, we developed an integrated analytical framework combining three spatially explicit GIS-based models. First, we employed a GIS overlay model—utilizing data on fire history, fuel flammability rankings, and proximity to roads—to identify high-danger zones. Within these zones, we tested two hypothetical scenarios: an undocumented immigrant traveling north toward a road and a border agent with communication devices capable of moving in any direction. To estimate evacuation timing, we developed a Pedestrian Mobility Model (PMM) using the Path Distance function in ArcGIS. The PMM integrated 30-m resolution digital elevation model (DEM) data, vegetation, and digitized road/trail networks, applying travel rate percentages based on slope and aspect to calculate least-accumulative cost travel times to safety zones. These travel times served as critical thresholds for the Wildland-Urban Interface Evacuation (WUIVAC) model, which utilized FlamMap fire spread parameters (including wind speed and direction) to generate evacuation trigger buffers (ETBs). By integrating the outputs of the overlay model, PMM, and WUIVAC, we generated wildfire risk maps to determine whether pedestrians in worst-case locations could reach safety before the arrival of a flaming front.
10.1080_00330120903546312: To examine the structural changes in Polish agriculture following the breakdown of socialism, we employed a multi-method approach combining quantitative statistical analysis, remote sensing, and qualitative interviews. Our study focused on Pyrzyce County in northwest Poland, a region characterized by a history of both state-owned and private family farming. We analyzed agricultural micro-census data from 1996 and 2002 to track changes in the total number of farms, the distribution of farm sizes (categorized as small, medium, and large), the prevalence of professional full-time farming, and total private farmland area. To assess the spatial manifestation of these changes, we utilized Landsat TM/ETM+ satellite images from June 1991 and June 2000, applying image segmentation to map individual fields and calculate changes in field size distributions and aggregation patterns. Finally, we conducted a survey consisting of 35 interviews with local farmers and 20 interviews with stakeholders to identify the socio-economic drivers and internal strategies associated with these shifts. By integrating fine-scale census data with spatial analysis and qualitative insights, we sought to explain the polarization of farm sizes and land use patterns within the context of emerging agricultural value chains.
10.1080_00330124.2010.483628: Drawing upon ethnographic and ethnobotanical literature from across the Amazon basin, with a particular emphasis on northwestern Amazonia, we examine the role of local seed systems in traditional indigenous and folk agricultural societies. We argue that the geographic, seasonal, and social scarcity of planting material—specifically seeds, cuttings, and pseudostems—shapes the socioeconomic relations and agricultural practices of rural Amazonian peoples. Our approach focuses on the material significance of planting stock access over symbolic or cultural dimensions, analyzing how the high bulk, perishability, and low stock-yield ratios of vegetatively propagated staples like manioc and plantain create distinct constraints compared to cereal crops. We analyze the mechanisms of acquisition, including the use of home gardens and swiddens as primary sources, as well as the operation of intra- and intercommunity exchange networks involving gifting, loans, and market transactions. By synthesizing data on crop-varietal diversity and the frequency of planting stock exchange, we demonstrate how these informal supply systems are critical to the maintenance of agrobiodiversity, the assurance of subsistence security for lowland farmers, and the facilitation of market product specialization among traditional farmers.
10.1080_00330124.2012.658713:
To assess whether the emergence and growth of ethnic minority groups in the Great Plains between 1970 and 2008 represents a localized phenomenon or a region-wide shift in population composition, we employ a multi-methodological approach utilizing quantitative spatial analysis. Our data are drawn from the 1970, 1980, 1990, and 2000 U.S. Decennial Censuses, the 2008 Population Estimates Program, the 2002 Census of Agriculture, and the Regional Economic Information System. We focus on five demographic groups—Latino, black, Asian, Native American, and white populations—across a study area encompassing the major regional delimitations of the Great Plains. 

To evaluate ethnic restructuring, we utilize choropleth maps alongside the Hoover and Simpson indexes; the former measures the concentration and dispersion of populations across the region, while the latter assesses the probability of diversity within individual counties. Additionally, we conduct a nonhierarchical cluster analysis to identify the characteristics of places experiencing demographic change. This analysis utilizes six categories of variables, including economic structure (employment percentages in manufacturing, agriculture, construction, wholesale-retail trade, and services), percentages of Asian and Latino populations, proximity to interstates, and the presence of universities or reservations. Statistical analysis was performed using SPSS (version 17.0) and mapping was conducted via ArcGIS (version 9.3.1).
10.1080_00330124.2012.681517: We utilized a combination of freely available Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) data and ground survey data to examine stream channel cross-section changes across an urban-to-rural land-use boundary in the North Carolina Piedmont. Our study area focused on two headwater streams, North and South Buffalo Creeks, within the Buffalo Creek watershed. Between 2005 and 2008, we conducted ground surveys of twenty-six channel cross sections across ten reaches on North Buffalo Creek to determine bankfull capacity, width, and depth. To extend these observations, we developed a digital topographic model using 3-m bare-earth LiDAR point sets and a 6.1-m hydrologically correct raster. We enhanced the model by manually digitizing top-of-bank breaklines from orthophotography and merging the 3-m data with raw, unclassified LiDAR point clouds to increase point density within the channels. We then calibrated the LiDAR-derived model by regressing modeled dimensions against field-surveyed data. This calibrated model allowed us to extract high-density cross-sectional data downstream, which we analyzed alongside independent hydraulic geometry curves and land-use mapping to evaluate the relationship between channel enlargement and the proportion of urbanized watershed area.
10.1080_00330124.2012.681584: We employ a case study approach to measure the temporal and spatial distribution of ranchette development in La Plata County, Colorado, an amenity-rich region characterized by high rates of recreation- and retirement-induced migration. To quantify land fragmentation, we utilize archival and contemporary land ownership data across three time intervals spanning a twenty-year period from 1988 to 2008. Following Colorado state statutes, we define ranchettes as rural parcels ranging from a minimum of 35 acres—the threshold for exemption from county subdivision regulations—to a maximum of 69.9 acres. This specific range ensures that the parcels are too small for viable commercial ranching yet large enough to avoid standard development codes. We supplement our county-level analysis with broader longitudinal data from the U.S. Department of Agriculture’s Census of Agriculture to contextualize La Plata County's trends within the wider patterns of "mountainous" Colorado counties. By analyzing changes in parcel size and ownership patterns, we aim to delineate the degree to which these low-density residential developments transform the rural landscape and identify the spatial mechanisms, such as contagious growth around initial development nodes, that drive exurban expansion.
10.1080_00330124.2012.700501: We evaluate the effect of moving window (MW) size on observed spatial fragmentation patterns and propose a method to identify an effective MW size using Simpson's diversity index. To test the robustness of this approach, we demonstrate its application across six cities in the Phoenix metropolitan area characterized by substantial variation in land composition and configuration. Our primary data sources are the 30-m resolution U.S. Geological Survey National Land Cover Data sets (NLCD) for 1992 and 2001, which we reclassify into three categories: developed, undeveloped, and cultivated. We test a series of MWs with side lengths ranging from 90 m to 2,370 m to determine the size that captures the maximum diversity of land classes. Additionally, we explore the effects of observation scale on urban-to-rural fragmentation gradients, comparing two popular approaches: concentric rings (at 1-km and 5-km widths) and transects (using gliding boxes of 15 km × 15 km and 15 km × 1 km). Through this methodology, we examine how the selection of window size and observation scale filters noise and influences the representation of land fragmentation patterns in a rapidly urbanizing region.
10.1080_00330124.2012.725373:
To understand the factors influencing decisions to return to rural U.S. communities and the barriers preventing such moves, we employed a multisited, interview-based qualitative methodology. Our research focused on demographically struggling and geographically isolated rural communities with limited natural amenities, specifically targeting individuals in their late twenties to late forties—a critical "settling down" life course segment where career and family obligations overlap and return migration typically peaks. 

To capture a comprehensive perspective, we conducted 309 interviews during 2008 and 2009 at twenty-seven high school reunions across twenty-one communities in seventeen states. High school reunions served as unique venues that allowed us to simultaneously engage with common cohorts of stayers, return migrants, and nonreturn migrants. Our analytical strategy integrated life course perspectives with social network theory, conceptualizing migration as a family-related and community-embedded process. By recording the personal views of these cohorts, we evaluated the complex interplay between economic circumstances, family incentives, and social and physical place settings to determine how ties to people and place operate in combination to drive or inhibit rural return migration.
10.1080_00330124.2014.886922:
In this study, we analyzed the diurnal spatial patterns of ozone ($\text{O}_3$) and respirable suspended particulate (RSP) matter in Hong Kong over an eleven-year period from 2001 to 2011. We utilized hourly air pollution data obtained from the Hong Kong Environmental Protection Department across fourteen monitoring stations, which were categorized into three groups: urban (including roadside), new town residential, and rural background stations. To reveal the diurnal cycles within seasonal-level spatial patterns, we employed harmonic analysis, aggregating data into four seasons: spring (March–May), summer (June–August), autumn (September–November), and winter (December–February). 

Using a harmonic equation, we estimated the air pollutant concentration frequency to determine the phase angle—interpreted as the time of maximum concentration—and the average hourly frequency. We further calculated the standardized amplitude to assess the relative importance of the diurnal cycle at each station and computed the portion of variance explained relative to the standard deviation of the twenty-four-hour concentration values. This methodological approach allowed for a comprehensive spatial analysis of pollutant behavior across the territory, accounting for the influence of vehicular traffic, titration effects, and the Asian monsoon system.
10.1080_00330124.2014.905158: Drawing on the theoretical frameworks of megapolitan geographies, urban political ecology, and urban metabolism, we theoretically and empirically explore the concept of "megapolitan political ecology." We first establish a conceptual framework tailored to southern Appalachia—specifically the Piedmont megapolitan region—positing that the megapolitan scale is uniquely suited to capture the urban metabolic connections and socio-spatial flows of people, resources, and capital that drive rapid urbanization in historically rural hinterlands. By integrating perspectives from exurban political ecology and the study of "global cities," we argue that these metabolic processes are central to the region's pressing socioecological crises. We then empirically ground this approach using data from the Coweeta Long-Term Ecological Research (LTER) program. Through the analysis of LTER data, we illustrate the human and nonhuman metabolisms across the Piedmont region, specifically highlighting the emergence of a "ring of asphalt" as a spatial manifestation of changing metabolic patterns. This methodology allows us to trace the circulation of matter and value across the urban-rural matrix, demonstrating how regional infrastructural networks and economic linkages transform the ecological and social vitality of southern Appalachia.
10.1080_00330124.2014.935165:
We examine the geography of fatal traffic collisions in Indiana from 2003 to 2011 using a multi-scalar spatial analysis to identify risk landscapes and patterns of mortality. To address the rarity of fatal events, we utilize a nine-year aggregation of data sourced from the Indiana State Police Automated Reporting Information Exchange System (ARIES), operationalizing the fatal collision rate as the percentage of all collisions resulting in at least one fatality. To mitigate the modifiable areal unit problem (MAUP), we employ three distinct spatial units of analysis: individual collision points, one-mile-square quadrats (36,879 grids) for density analysis, and ninety-two Indiana counties. 

Our methodological approach integrates several spatial statistical techniques. We first analyze point patterns and compare risks across road types, locality classifications, and responding law enforcement agencies. We then apply the nearest neighbor index (NNI) to determine if statewide fatal collision points are dispersed, clustered, or random. To assess spatial interaction and dependence among counties, we calculate the global Moran's I for statewide spatial autocorrelation and the local Moran's I to identify specific clusters of positive (high-high, low-low) or negative (low-high, high-low) autocorrelation. These analyses were conducted using ArcGIS v.10.0 and Stata v.12.1.
10.1080_00330124.2015.1028324:
We examine household-level characteristics that predict water insecurity in low-income rural and periurban communities, specifically *colonias*, located in Hidalgo and El Paso Counties along the Texas-Mexico border. Our approach integrates qualitative and quantitative methods to operationalize water security as a multidimensional construct comprising water access, quality, and water affect. We first conducted semistructured interviews and focus groups in Hidalgo County to inform the development of a household water security survey. This survey was administered to households across various *colonia* classifications (red, yellow, green, and unclassified) to capture a representative range of infrastructural conditions.

To analyze the data, we employed an experiential scalogram technique to draw inferences about the latent variable of water insecurity across the three identified dimensions. Using the resulting scale scores, we clustered households into four categories—water secure, marginally water secure, marginally water insecure, and water insecure—via the centroid method and squared Euclidean distance. Finally, we employed two logistic regression models (binary and ordered) to identify which household characteristics, including socioeconomic factors and immigration status, significantly predict the likelihood of household water insecurity.
10.1080_00330124.2015.1062700:
I apply a historical political ecology framework to examine municipal drinking water governance in colonial Lima, Peru, between 1578 and 1700. My objective is to analyze how power dynamics influenced the sociospatial patterns of water access and control, and how these patterns reinforced colonial social divisions and shaped urban-rural relations. To achieve this, I combine theoretical themes from the political ecology of water—specifically unequal access, urban bias, and the concept of "archipelagos" of supply—with the multidisciplinary methods of hydraulic archaeology. 

Because the city's extensive urban development has obscured most physical remains of colonial infrastructure, I rely primarily on archival analysis. My central data source is the *Libros de Cabildos de Lima* (LCL), the minutes of the City Council, which provide a continuous record of municipal actions. I analyze all references within these records to springs, reservoirs, pipelines, and petitions for water connections to reconstruct the material aspects of Lima's hydraulic space. By mapping pipeline routes and the locations of public and private connections, I evaluate the spatial and temporal distribution of water and the role of the *Cabildo* in managing resource inequality during the period when the city's primary potable water infrastructure was established.
10.1080_00330124.2015.1065545: We employ a sociospatial framework to investigate the interaction between social network position and natural resource access among households in four rural communities in South Africa (Tokwe and Muncu in Willowvale; Sewefontein and Zaaimanshoek in Baviaanskloof). Our methodology integrates social network analysis (SNA) with geographic position data obtained via Global Positioning System (GPS), satellite imagery, and participatory mapping. To quantify social embeddedness, we calculate four measures of network centrality—degree, betweenness, closeness, and eigenvector centrality—based on household exchanges of food, money, firewood, water, manual labor, and disease information. These social variables are analyzed alongside geographic variables, specifically the mean and summed area of polygons encompassing natural resources (wood, water, bush medicine, and livestock grazing), as well as the mean and sum distance from households to these resource polygons. By combining these datasets at the household level, we test for statistical associations between an actor's topological position within the community network and their spatial proximity to essential natural resources, seeking to determine if social relations bear upon spatiality or vice versa within these marginalized rural contexts.
10.1080_00330124.2016.1157499: To investigate the geographical and social dimensions of food access in rural Cuba, we employed a mixed-methods approach centered on the use of food diaries. Our study was conducted in March 2015 within a rural community in the province of Sancti Spiritus, specifically targeting forty households across two adjacent Committees for the Defense of the Revolution (CDRs) to capture varying levels of connection to local agricultural cooperatives (CPAs and CCSs). Following a pilot study with five core families to identify primary food sources, we distributed detailed worksheets to all forty households, requiring them to record everything they ate and the specific origin of each ingredient for one full week. To ensure data reliability and accuracy, we conducted daily visits to the participating households. This quantitative tracking of food items and sources—ranging from state-run bodegas and markets to individual gardens and informal gifts—was integrated with qualitative data gathered through interviews and participant observation. By combining these methods, we aimed to move beyond the "spatial-only" limitations of GIS-based food desert research to uncover the complex, relational networks and informal social relations that facilitate food access in a non-market-based rural context.
10.1080_00330124.2016.1198264: I develop and deepen the concept of the rural-urban interface through a mixed-method, comparative case study conducted in Calaveras County, California. Situated in the Sierra Nevada foothills, this study area serves as a representative site of rural restructuring, characterized by exurban residential development, amenity migration, and the pressures of the urbanizing Central Valley. Drawing from a larger study of divergent land management outcomes, I utilize a specific case of contested ecologies to (re)conceptualize the rural-urban interface as a multidimensional construct of meaning, model, and metaphor (MMM). My empirical approach, conducted between January and June 2010, integrated participant observation, a short political-ideological survey, and semi-structured, in-depth interviews with fifty-one key stakeholders, including ranchers, farmers, business owners, community activists, public servants, and planners. By synthesizing these qualitative and quantitative data with extant scholarship on exurbia, I examine how the interface functions not merely as a spatial boundary, but as a conduit for sociocultural, ecological, financial, and material flows. This methodology allows me to situate contrasting people and places within an ongoing negotiation of environmental meaning, arguing that perceived boundaries are instrumental for understanding physical-material change and mitigating social conflicts along the urbanizing edge.
10.1080_00330124.2017.1365308: I introduce an inverted two-step floating catchment area (i2SFCA) method, derived from an extension of the classic Huff model, to measure the "crowdedness"—defined as resource scarcity or competition intensity—of service facilities. While the regular 2SFCA is resident-oriented and assesses the ease of obtaining a service, the i2SFCA switches the supply and demand terms to examine the supply side. I illustrate and validate this approach through a case study of hospital inpatient services in Florida, utilizing data from the 2011 State Inpatient Databases (SID). The dataset includes 268 hospitals (supply capacity defined by staffed bed size), 983 ZIP code areas (demand defined by the number of patients generated), and road network travel times. To determine the distance decay function, I analyzed patient flow patterns, ultimately employing an exponential function with a distance decay parameter of $b = 0.14$. I further examine the connection between accessibility and crowdedness by using a spatially adaptive filter to compute average accessibility within a window of the five nearest ZIP code areas around each hospital, allowing for a scale-consistent comparison between resident-based and facility-oriented metrics.
10.1080_00330124.2017.1365309: Drawing on the Vermont Roots Migration Project, we employed a mixed-methods approach to explore the residential decision-making processes of rural stayers. We utilized an online anonymous survey distributed via social media networks to capture the narratives of individuals who spent at least a portion of their youth in Vermont. The survey gathered demographic data—including gender, education, and occupation—and required respondents to select from a list of reasons for staying while providing open-text narrative explanations for their residential choices. From an initial pool of 3,692 responses, we focused on a data set of 3,360 individuals aged 25 and older, specifically isolating "contented stayers"—those who reported enjoying life in Vermont and had not lived outside the state for more than one year (excluding schooling or military service). Our analysis proceeded in two stages: first, we conducted a statistical analysis using SPSS to perform crosstab analyses and chi-square tests on migration factors across groups of contentment, gender, and educational attainment. Second, we adopted a biographical approach, identifying specific respondent profiles and analyzing their open-text narratives to construct biographical sketches. This methodology allowed us to join structural patterns with individual life experiences to uncover the emotional and place-based dimensions of staying.
10.1080_00330124.2018.1443479:
This case study examines collective strategies for sustaining organizational resilience among nonprofit organizations in the wake of the 2010 and 2011 earthquake sequence in Christchurch, New Zealand. Undertaken three to four years after the most severe events, the study utilizes a retrospective approach to model nonprofit collaboration during midterm recovery. Data were collected in late 2014 from thirty-six social service-oriented nonprofits—comprising thirty-four organizations and two agency connection groups—recruited via the Community Information Network Christchurch website and snowball sampling. Inclusion criteria required formal organization status, location within the central business district or suburbs, receipt of philanthropic funds, and a social service mission.

The methodological framework employed semistructured interviews with managers from each entity, focus groups with staff from five selected organizations to capture size-based variances, and semistructured reviews of staff responses. Participants were asked to reflect longitudinally on changes in operations and partnerships dating back to two years prior to the disaster. A cross-case analysis was then conducted, with transcripts themed according to nonprofit management, emergency management, and national governance literature. By analyzing longitudinal and categorization patterns—such as field of work, date of emergence (preexisting vs. emergent), and affiliation—the study quantitatively and qualitatively evaluated shifts in partnership participation and service demands to propose a scalable model for sustaining network resilience.
10.1080_00330124.2018.1453184: In this article, I employ a situated and positioned personal account to illustrate the diversity of community-engaged and activist scholarship within geography. Eschewing a formal debate on the boundaries of activist research, I instead share two contrasting narratives of engagement to demonstrate how such opportunities often emerge organically at the intersection of personal ethics, commitments, and research agendas. The first case involves the establishment of Los Puentes, a Spanish-English dual-language immersion program in a low-income rural public school system in eastern North Carolina during the early 2000s, where I served as a program founder, scholar, and parent. The second case describes my collaboration on the "Building Austin, Building Injustice" project in Austin, Texas, a participatory action research (PAR) initiative conducted with the Proyecto de Defensa Laboral (Workers Defense Project). In the latter, a community-based workers center maintained ownership and control over the research design and data collection to document labor conditions and lobby for construction worker protections. By reflecting on these experiences, I seek to highlight the potential for activist scholarship to emerge outside conventional arenas and to discuss the professional challenges and opportunities for geographers committed to grounded, social-justice-oriented praxis.
10.1080_00330124.2018.1455524: We engage with findings from an ongoing research project evaluating the impacts of flooding variability on rural livelihoods and environmental perceptions in the Okavango Delta of Botswana. To analyze the region as a hydrosocial waterscape, we employ an integrated mixed-methods approach combining qualitative and quantitative techniques, including semistructured interviewing, remote sensing analysis, vegetation transects, and livelihood mapping. Between 2010 and 2016, we conducted 131 household interviews in the villages of Seronga, Mababe, and Etsha 1, 6, and 13. We utilized a spatially stratified random sampling procedure to account for the role of spatial location in determining household exposure to flooding variability. Specifically, the Etsha villages were selected to provide a social transect for examining microvariations in flooding extent. Data were processed using ATLAS.ti to code and categorize themes related to environmental dynamics and dominant livelihood systems. Our sampling strategy targeted three sites with distinct flooding regimes—ranging from regular seasonal fluctuations in Seronga to subdecadal changes in the Etsha villages and decadal change in Mababe—allowing us to examine how different temporal frequencies of environmental change shape resource access and governance.
10.1080_00330124.2018.1501707:
I argue that traditional cultural or economic regionalizations of the American West are limited by a reliance on state or county boundaries and a failure to account for spatial patterns of land management and ownership. To address this, I propose a new conceptual framework that divides the region into three distinct "Wests" based on opportunities for economic activity and population growth: the Booming West, the Protected West, and the Bypassed West. 

To map these regions, I define the Booming West using 2010 Census data, aggregating urbanized clusters of at least 1,500 people. The Protected West is delineated using the Protected Areas Database of the U.S. (PADUS), encompassing national park units, national forests, military bases, Department of Energy lands, wildlife refuges, Indian reservations, and specific BLM designations such as national monuments and Areas of Critical Environmental Concern. The Bypassed West is defined residually as all remaining land not captured by the first two categories, including private land outside booming areas and general BLM holdings. By synthesizing these data sources, I move beyond administrative boundaries to highlight the sharp transitions and interrelationships between areas of rapid growth, legal preservation, and economic stagnation.
10.1080_00330124.2018.1559653: We investigate the relationship between regional innovation and income inequality using a panel dataset of Chinese city regions from 2004 to 2012. To capture the heterogeneity of the Chinese landscape, we utilize the city region scale rather than provincial data, categorizing the study area into eastern, northeastern, central, and western regions. We employ a regression model where income inequality—measured both as the ratio of urban per capita disposable income to rural per capita net income and as the ratio of the urban-rural income difference to city region per capita income—is estimated as a function of innovation and several control variables, including industrial structure upgrades, the proportion of primary sector activities, market openness, human capital, GDP per capita, and city size. Innovation is proxied by the number of patent applications per million residents. To test for a nonlinear relationship, we incorporate the square term of innovation into our model. Furthermore, we apply endogenous threshold effect models to determine if GDP per capita exerts a triple-threshold effect on the influence of innovation on inequality.
