To further test the geographical concentration of this growth, we calculated a Hoover index to measure the spatial focus of population change across the identified clusters, determining the extent to which migration is concentrated within high-amenity, service-based counties.
10.1111_0033-0124.00304:
To investigate land-use changes in a rural-urban environment over a thirty-five-year period (1958–1993), we developed a programming package within the MapInfo environment using MapBasic language to build temporal topology in a GIS database. Our study area covers 960 ha in the municipality of St-Eustache, Quebec. We constructed a georeferenced spatiotemporal database by producing a series of eight land-use snapshots from aerial photographs (scales 1:10,000 to 1:35,000) at approximately five-year intervals, which were then integrated with digitized cadastral and zoning maps. 

To move beyond the limitations of the snapshot model, we created a space-time composite map by superimposing subsequent land-use maps onto a 1958 template, decomposing the area into polygons with distinct attribute histories. We implemented concepts of version and mutation to establish temporal topology, utilizing variables such as type, beginning, end, duration, and uncertainty markers (beginning min, end max, and duration max) to characterize each object's evolution. This approach allowed us to perform complex spatiotemporal queries using temporal and logical operators to extract the rate of change, land-use succession patterns, and the relationship between land-use transitions and zoning regulations.
10.1111_0033-0124.00323:
To assess the degree of financial "dependence" of forest peoples on forest resources, I analyzed market activity among the Tawahka Sumu of eastern Honduras within the Tawahka Asangni Biosphere Reserve. Drawing on a multiyear dataset, I utilized three household-level surveys: one conducted by the Harvard Institute for International Development in 1995 and two conducted by myself in 1996 and 1998. I organized these data into two distinct sets: a cross-sectional analysis of market production (1997–1998) for approximately 100 households across five villages, and a longitudinal analysis covering three years (1994, 1995, 1997) for a subset of 49 households from a single village. 

I categorized market income into seven sectors, combining geographical and functional criteria to distinguish own-account production—such as the sale of wild-harvested forest products—from wage labor and other business activities. Key variables included the gross value of goods and services sold or exchanged, with specific attention paid to the disaggregation of "farm" and "forest" sectors to isolate direct investments in resource marketing. By analyzing this data across nested spatial scales (group, village, and household) and temporal scales, I sought to demonstrate how the aggregation of economic data conditions the characterization of forest-dependent livelihoods.
10.1111_0033-0124.00336: To investigate the growth of business services and their relationship with local economic conditions in Michigan, I developed three models analyzing the interplay between business-service industries—defined specifically as Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) 73—and three key local economic indicators: overall average wages, manufacturing wages, and manufacturing productivity. The study employs a comparative approach to determine if the economic trajectories of metropolitan and nonmetropolitan counties diverge, testing the hypothesis that an expanded division of labor has resulted in two distinct economic geographies. Using county-level data from 1977 to 1997, the analysis focuses on the spatial distribution of SIC73 employment across Michigan's industrial corridors and peripheral regions. By modeling these variables, I examine whether the localized benefits associated with the business-services sector, such as increased productivity and wage growth, are more strongly associated with metropolitan economies than with nonmetropolitan ones, while also tracking how the interdependencies between these services and the manufacturing sector have evolved over the twenty-year period.
10.1111_0033-0124.00349:
I adopt an explorative approach to analyze the sources of urban and rural population growth in Estonia during the Soviet period (1945–1991), seeking empirical evidence to evaluate universalist versus specific theories of urbanization under central planning. Utilizing data from the Estonian Statistical Office (ESO), I examine population totals, vital statistics, age composition, and migration records. To account for improvements in data quality and shifts in growth drivers, I divide the analysis into two distinct timeframes: 1946–1959 and 1959–1991.

The methodology involves decomposing population change into three primary constituent variables: administrative changes (specifically the reclassification of settlements), net migration (further disaggregated into internal rural-to-urban flows and external immigration), and natural increase. By quantifying these components, I assess how the interplay of industrialization, Soviet ideological mandates, and the systemic inefficiencies of a shortage economy influenced the spatial distribution of the population. This longitudinal analysis allows me to compare Estonia's urbanization curve against broader cross-country patterns while identifying specific Soviet legacies that shaped the country's demographic landscape leading up to the postsocialist transition.
10.1111_1467-8306.00288: We evaluate the utility of radiance-calibrated Defense Meteorological Satellite Program (DMSP)-Operational Linescan System (OLS) nighttime images for extracting urban indicators in China. Using a composite of cloud-free images acquired between March 1996 and February 1997 in 30-arc-second grids, we focus on thirty-five major cities, ensuring representation from each province. With the aid of image-processing and ArcView GIS software, we reproject the data into a Lambert conic conformal projection and employ the 3-D Analysis extension to create Triangulated Irregular Network (TIN) models for each city. By treating digital numbers (DN) as altitudes, we extract two key variables from these "illuminated urban area" (IUA) domes: surface area and volume. These variables are then used as independent variables in allometric growth models to estimate nonagricultural population, gross domestic product (GDP), built-up area, and electricity consumption. We validate these estimates against 1997 data from the Statistical Bureau of China. Our approach allows us to assess the correlation between light intensity and socioeconomic indicators while examining the capacity of low-resolution nighttime imagery to reveal city morphology and the phenomenon of extended metropolitan regions.
10.1111_1467-8306.93110: We initiate a critical analysis of the discursive geographies underpinning U.S. agricultural legislation, employing a poststructuralist framework to examine how farming and farmers are socially constructed. Our methodological approach is bifurcated along two dimensions. First, we analyze the "geography of discourse"—the production and dissemination of ideas—by tracing a partial genealogy of American agrarianism from the nineteenth through the twentieth centuries. We identify key discursive sites, the role of expert groups, and the "upscaling" of regional socioeconomic contexts into national federal policy. Second, we examine the "semantic geography" of discourse, focusing on the use of binaries (e.g., family/corporate, welfare/investment) to valorize specific norms. To operationalize this, we focus on the U.S. Senate as a primary discursive site, specifically analyzing the debates surrounding the 1996 Freedom to Farm Bill. Our data sources include verbatim transcripts and news summaries from the *Congressional Digest* and the *Congressional Quarterly Weekly* covering the period between June 1995 and April 1996. Through this analysis, we explore how farming is spatially situated within the constructs of the marketplace, the rural, and the United States to justify legislative shifts toward neoliberal market orientation.
10.1111_gere.12007: In this long-form essay, I employ a method I term "geographical portraiture," utilizing a single, scenic landmark—the scarecrow—as a cipher to explore the broader narratives of agricultural society, rural values, and landscape politics. My approach begins with what might be described as an "unsystematic transect," conducting opportunistic field observations from the vantage point of cross-country rail travel. By utilizing the picture windows of railway carriages as frames for a rapid scan of the rural scene, I sought to identify the presence or absence of traditional bird-scaring contraptions across the British countryside. This observational data is then woven together with a cultural history of the scarecrow, drawing upon literary references, folk art archives, and regional dialect maps to situate the object within the traditions of vernacular art and "native survivalism." To deepen the search, I transitioned from passive rail observation to active, haphazard fieldwork in the arable shires, utilizing Ordnance Survey maps and binoculars to conduct a "missing-person hunt" for the scarecrow. Through this blend of subjective, affective writing and opportunistic sampling, I examine the scarecrow not merely as a functional deterrent, but as a threshold figure reflecting the tensions between human and nonhuman lives and the shifting nature of the pastoral myth.
10.1111_gere.12226:
We analyze the perspectives of economic-development stakeholders on boomtown dynamics within the Eagle Ford Shale of south Texas, focusing specifically on the impacts regarding roads, wages, and housing. To capture these local, place-based development characteristics, we employed a semistructured interview framework, purposively sampling respondents from four key policymaking organizations: chambers of commerce, local development corporations, county governments, and municipal policymakers. We conducted eleven face-to-face interview sessions between August 2014 and March 2015, a period encompassing the peak and subsequent decline of global crude oil prices. To account for the volatility of this period, we conducted follow-up and new interviews with stakeholders in June 2016. 

Our methodological approach utilizes a grounded theory framework to transcribe and code interviews via Atlas.ti, allowing emergent themes to structure our findings. We use these data to describe and analyze boomtown processes through the lens of Gilmore’s 1976 "problem triangle" model. By integrating these stakeholder views, we extend and amplify Gilmore's foundational model to incorporate the specific materiality of unconventional hydrocarbon extraction—such as long-distance commuting and contemporary recovery processes—thereby generating updated theoretical insights and testable predictions for other energy-impacted communities.
10.1111_gere.12244: As part of the Peruvian Amazon Rural Livelihoods and Poverty (PARLAP) project, we conducted a large-scale census of 174 Amerindian and *ribereño* communities and towns within the Napo River basin in northeastern Peru between 2013 and 2014. Our methodological approach employed social network theory to examine the structure and spatial configuration of intercommunity connections, specifically focusing on three types of social interaction: crop seed acquisition, cooperative labor sharing, and intercommunity soccer matches. Data were gathered through focus-group interviews using a structured questionnaire administered by a local field team traveling by boat across the basin. We identified "partner" communities based on the flow of seeds and labor, as well as the frequency of soccer matches, while recording community locations via GPS. By organizing these linkages into matrices for relational analysis, we analyzed the sociospatial networks of each practice. Our analysis specifically tested for effects of homophily—the propensity for ties to form between communities of the same ethnicity—and the presence of "weak ties" or bridging interactions between upland (*terra firme*) and lowland (floodplain) communities, while accounting for the influence of distance and settlement patterns.
10.1111_gere.12245: To identify and illustrate the manifestations of "virtual rurality" and the "rural idyll" within the American imagination, we employ a blend of qualitative and quantitative research methods. Our approach frames the phenomenon not as attachment to a specific location, but as an attachment to a generic type of place, drawing on psychological concepts of generic place attachment and geographical theories of the rural idyll. We gather qualitative evidence through field observations, a review of existing literature, and computer searches to identify visible portrayals of idealized rurality in popular culture, including advertising for real estate, lifestyles, and recreational opportunities. To complement these qualitative findings, we incorporate quantitative data from the U.S. Census of Agriculture (specifically comparing 1992 and 2012 data) to track trends in small-scale and micro-farming across various U.S. regions. While we do not follow a singular discourse analysis, our synthesis of these diverse data sources allows us to categorize six primary embodiments of the rural ideal—ranging from agritourism and hobby farming to urban agriculture and symbolic naming conventions—across a spatial scale that extends from deep rural areas to peri-urban and urban environments.
10.1111_gere.12247: To examine the intersection of aquaculture expansion and the postproductive transition on the Maine coast, I conducted a comparative analysis of three primary aquaculture regions: the Damariscotta River, Cobscook Bay, and the area surrounding Blue Hill Bay. I conceptualized these regions as sites of rural restructuring, characterized by a shift from commodity production toward amenity-driven consumption and recreation. My primary data sources consisted of aquaculture lease hearing transcripts from the Maine Department of Marine Resources spanning 1981 to 2015. Of the 263 available transcripts, I analyzed 90 that included public hearings. Using NVivo11, I and two assistants employed inductive coding to quantify and categorize the specific concerns raised by the public—primarily coastal landowners—during these proceedings. I further bifurcated these hearings into "uncertainty" and "NIMBY" (Not In My Back Yard) groups based on the level of conflict and the stridency of objections. By analyzing variables such as navigation obstacles, environmental impacts, and aesthetic concerns, I sought to understand how divergent regional histories and geographies influence the negotiation between aquaculture farmers and amenity migrants in a postproductive landscape.
10.1111_gere.12264: We conducted a qualitative, exploratory study of country stores in rural Vermont to investigate how these built environments function as socio-spatial nodes within multifunctional rural landscapes. Our research, carried out in 2015 and 2016, focused on seventeen stores across fifteen towns, ranging in population from 298 to 5,009 residents. These sites represented a diverse array of local economies, including commuter towns and amenity-rich tourism areas. To gather data, we employed a multi-method approach consisting of site visits using a standardized data-collection form to document physical layout, geographic location, and merchandise; semistructured interviews with storekeepers and key stakeholders, including the director of a historic preservation organization; and an open-text online survey administered to community members attempting to establish a cooperative store. We specifically distinguished "country stores"—independently owned, long-standing establishments—from franchise convenience stores to isolate the specific socio-material roles of the former. By analyzing the range of goods, formal and informal services, and community engagements, we sought to identify the reflexive relationships between these stores and the broader physical and cultural geographies of their regions.
10.1111_gere.12287:
This comparative investigation examines the relationships between primary sector value chains and rural development in the Philippines, specifically focusing on four export-oriented products: bananas, coconut oil, tuna, and seaweed/carrageenan. To address the gap in literature regarding how smallholders and fishers are inserted into these chains, I employ an analytical framework that integrates the Sustainable Livelihoods Approach (SLA) with Global Value Chain (GVC) analysis. This integration allows for a multi-scalar examination of the "capitals pentagon," the vulnerability context, and the institutional political economy, facilitating an analysis of both horizontal and vertical coordination and the resulting trajectories of product and process upgrading.

The empirical analysis relies primarily on a comprehensive review of secondary sources, including academic literature, reports from consultants and NGOs, and Philippine media, to map value-chain structures and rural livelihood challenges. To supplement this, I conducted primary fieldwork in Guimaras Province in 2016, specifically targeting the seaweed value chain. This fieldwork comprised sixteen semi-structured interviews with key informants across six barangays and a survey of thirty-four seaweed-growing households on the island barangay of Panobolon. By combining these data sources, the study evaluates how changing value-chain dynamics impact the livelihoods of approximately 3 million households and identifies the systemic barriers to upstream upgrading.
10.1111_gere.12321:
We conducted this study in the Barcelona Metropolitan Region (BMR), a polycentric area in northeast Spain characterized by a mix of dense urban cores and low-density rural environments. To identify territorial disparities in youth mobility, we analyzed data from the 2014 Workday Mobility Survey (EMEF'14), which utilized Computer Assisted Telephone Interviews (CATI) with 9,461 residents. We focused our analysis on a subsample of 1,063 young adults aged eighteen to twenty-nine years, comparing their everyday mobility patterns against a reference population of older adults aged thirty to sixty-four. 

To examine the effect of territorial configuration, we categorized municipalities as either rural (under 10,000 inhabitants) or urban (over 50,000 inhabitants). We adopted a comprehensive perspective by analyzing all trip purposes—distinguishing between occupational (work/study) and personal trips—and all transportation modes, including active, private, and public transit. Key variables included trip engagement, modal split, and daily travel time expenditures. We employed cross-tabulation with Chi-squared tests and t-tests to determine significant differences in average behavior between age groups and territorial categories, performing all statistical analyses using IBM SPSS v21.
10.1111_gere.12366: To analyze the impact of social connections and contextual factors on the sense of belonging among rural-to-urban migrants in China, we conducted an empirical study utilizing data from the 2013 National Migrant Population Dynamic Monitoring Survey (MDMS). Our sample comprised 12,807 migrants aged 15–59 across eight purposively selected cities (including Shanghai, Wuhan, and Xi'an), drawn from approximately 750 neighborhoods using a three-stage probability proportionate to size (PPS) sampling technique. We measured the dependent variable, sense of belonging, by applying Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to six Likert-scale items covering place attachment, identity, and social bonding. To quantify social contacts, we categorized interactions into four groups: nonresidents, kin residents, nonkin residents, and local authorities. We further incorporated contextual variables, such as city population size and the availability of public services, alongside individual-level proxies for ties to the place of origin, including remittance amounts and landholding. To account for the nested nature of the data, we implemented multilevel linear regression models (MLMs), employing intraclass correlation (ICC) and likelihood ratio tests to validate the necessity of a multilevel approach over a single-level model.
10.1111_gere.12367: We investigate the development trajectories and organizational structures of Taobao villages in Zhejiang Province, China, to better understand the intersection of e-commerce and regional development. Our methodological approach combines quantitative data from AliResearch reports with primary empirical data collected through field research. In July 2016, we conducted surveys of online enterprises across 22 Taobao villages and industrial parks located in the cities of Hangzhou, Yiwu, and Lishui. Of the 269 survey forms personally delivered to online enterprises, we received 254 effective returns, yielding a return rate of 94.42 percent. To complement these surveys, we conducted in-depth interviews with a diverse range of local actors, including entrepreneurs, representatives from e-commerce trade associations, and village government officers. By synthesizing these data sources, we examine the roles of local states, ICT firms, and spatial networking in shaping the restructuring of rural development models. This mixed-methods approach allows us to scrutinize how rural e-tailers are embedded within broader production networks and how local contexts influence the transition from traditional rural economies to e-commerce-driven regional growth.
10.1111_j.0033-0124.1990.00471.x:
We explain the computer implementation of the mile marker addressing system across several counties in New Mexico to improve the location of rural residences. Our approach utilizes a public-private cooperative model where county governments provide personnel for field work and sign posting, while New Mexico State University provides computer mapping expertise and database construction. We collect data from two primary sources: mapped data, including USGS orthophoto quadrangles (1975–1982) with Public Land Survey System (PLSS) overlays and 7.5-minute topographic maps, and field survey data gathered through house-to-house interviews and observations. 

Using a manual digitizer and the state plane coordinate system, we enter road networks, dwellings, and political boundaries into a geographic database. This process involves an iterative field editing cycle where county personnel verify plotted maps for errors of omission or misalignment before final redigitization. The resulting system integrates geographic base files with a relational survey database containing resident information. We deliver these tools—including IBM-PC compatible hardware, specialized software, and a rural addressing atlas reduced to a scale of one inch to the mile—alongside technical training in cartography and software use to ensure local sustainability and integration with county clerk and assessor records.
10.1111_j.0033-0124.1993.00012.x:
To examine the conflict between low-wage employment and high housing costs in the amenity town of Sedona, Arizona, we conducted a survey of the community's workforce in August 1991. Utilizing a systematic sampling approach, we selected every third business from the Sedona Chamber of Commerce membership list, distributing questionnaires to 64 businesses and 846 employees. From these, we obtained 286 completed responses (a 33.8% response rate). We categorized respondents into three groups based on their residence/workplace patterns: employees residing within Sedona city limits, those in the suburban Village of Oak Creek, and long-distance commuters from the broader laborshed, including towns such as Cottonwood, Clarkdale, Cornville, and Camp Verde. 

Our analysis focused on comparing these groups across several key variables, including demographic characteristics (age, marital status, number of children), socioeconomic indicators (household income, homeownership rates), and housing costs (mean property values and monthly rents). We employed ANOVA and chi-square tests to determine the statistical significance of differences between the groups. By analyzing the tradeoffs between commuting time and housing expenditures, we sought to evaluate the relationship between income levels and residential location within the context of a nonmetropolitan amenity environment.
10.1111_j.0033-0124.1994.00178.x: This paper employs a dual retrospective and prospective approach to analyze the processes of agricultural restructuring, focusing primarily on the Canadian context. I begin with an empirical analysis of structural change in Canadian agriculture from 1971 to 1991, utilizing time series data from Statistics Canada to examine key variables such as farm numbers, average farm size, gross receipts, and debt-to-asset ratios. This quantitative assessment is used to identify trends of concentration, polarization, and financial instability within the sector. I then situate these empirical findings within a broader theoretical framework by synthesizing and comparing six contemporary development models of advanced capitalist economies, ranging from neoclassical "induced technical change" and political economy perspectives on capital penetration and uneven development to models emphasizing human agency and societal-environmental dialectics. By integrating this empirical evidence with a review of existing theoretical literature, I examine the role of national and international policy in shaping the current agricultural landscape and explore alternative development paths toward sustainable food and rural systems.
10.1111_j.0033-0124.1994.00199.x:
We employ location-allocation models to evaluate the spatial accessibility of health care facilities in the Suhum District of Ghana, specifically testing the "add-more" policy recommendation against the alternative of optimizing facility location. Using 1984 census data, we identify 150 population centers as demand points, with population size serving as the proxy for demand. Because walking is the primary mode of travel, we utilize a distance-generating algorithm to calculate Euclidean distances between locations. 

Our methodological approach treats the three-level hierarchical health system (A, B, and C levels) as successively inclusive. We apply the p-median criterion to minimize average weighted travel distance for A-level services, comparing the actual system of 30 facilities against an optimal configuration. To assess the potential for improving accessibility without increasing resources, we vary the number of facilities ($p$) from 1 to 30 to determine the point of diminishing returns. Additionally, we utilize the maximum coverage criterion to evaluate the supervisory capacity of B-level facilities over A-level facilities, specifically testing the government's goal of maintaining a B-level facility within 8 km of every A-level facility. The Tietz and Bart heuristic algorithm is used to solve these location-allocation problems.
10.1111_j.0033-0124.1995.00148.x: To address the paucity of statistical data regarding the cottage-owning population, we employ a multiple case study approach focusing on three distinct Canadian regions: the Rideau Lakes of Ontario, the Cultus Lake area of British Columbia, and the Beaubassin coastline of New Brunswick. These sites were selected to capture regional economic variations and represent the "rural-recreational countryside" within the broader urban field. Our methodology integrates two primary data sources to examine property distribution, ownership patterns, and socioeconomic profiles. First, we utilize local government property assessment and taxation records to identify seasonal-use cottage properties and determine the permanent residence of owners. Second, we administered a questionnaire survey in the summer of 1991 to a random sample of seasonal-use cottage owners and year-round rural residents. The sampling frame for each area was determined by the number of cottage-to-year-round conversions identified over the preceding decade. By comparing these two resident groups—defined by settlement type and occupancy pattern rather than a standard farm/non-farm dichotomy—we analyze key variables including age, education, and income to test assumptions regarding the socioeconomic differences between cottagers and local rural residents.
10.1111_j.0033-0124.1995.00159.x:
We examined the relationship between human activities and forest-landscape structure along a 20-km wide, 140-km long belt transect extending from the urban core of New York City to rural northwestern Connecticut. To control for environmental variability, we positioned the transect within the glaciated Appalachian highlands and the oak-hickory vegetation association. We quantified urban structures and processes at both coarse (1:250,000) and fine (1:24,000) resolutions, utilizing data from the U.S. Bureau of Census, USGS planimetric and topographic maps, traffic volume reports, and aerial photography (1967–1991). Key urban variables included population density, annual growth rates, traffic volume, road length, and urban-residential land proportions. 

Using raster-based geographic information systems (IDRISI and MAP) and specialized spatial analysis software, we measured forest-landscape structure through parameters such as mean patch size, patch number, perimeter-area fractal dimensions for shape complexity, and the percentage of forest edges adjacent to urban land uses. We employed linear and logarithmic regressions to document trends in urban activity relative to distance from the city core and used correlations and partial correlations to determine the extent to which urban structures and processes explain variation in the distribution, size, and shape of native forest patches.
10.1111_j.0033-0124.1995.00411.x: In this study, I employ a qualitative research approach to explore the complexities of spontaneous intra-rural migration in Sri Lanka, arguing that personal narratives can capture individual decision-making processes and contextual nuances that quantitative methods often overlook. Moving away from traditional macro-level statistical analyses, I utilize the narrative method—defined here as the collection and interpretation of individuals' stories—to illuminate the intersection of human agency and structural constraints. To exemplify this approach, I present a case study of "Amma," a female rural migrant. The data for this narrative were gathered through a series of informal interviews conducted via an interpreter, supplemented by my own field observations and accounts provided by the subject's son. Rather than a comprehensive life history, I focus specifically on the story of her migration to reveal the role of gender, family ties, and the specific socio-spatial characteristics of the rural Sri Lankan context. By treating the narrative as both a phenomenon and a method, I aim to achieve verisimilitude, providing a grounded, idiographic account that links individual action to the broader social and geographical environment.
10.1111_j.0033-0124.1996.00070.x:
I examined the use of urban and rural habitats by eleven small arboreal bird species common to pine woodlands in the vicinity of Athens, Georgia, during the winters of 1989-1990 and 1990-1991. To determine population densities, I conducted a series of censuses along straight-line transects at four primary sites—two rural (Whitehall Forest and the Botanical Garden) and two urban residential developments (Pine View Drive and Davis Estates). I compared species densities between rural and urban sites using one-tailed Mann-Whitney U-tests and performed detectability analyses to ensure that distance from transects did not bias the results.

To assess habitat use, I mapped the primary sites into twelve categories based on canopy closure, dominant tree life form, and understory openness. I then used one-tailed $\chi^2$ tests with Yates' correction to determine if birds avoided highly disturbed habitats, such as those with open canopies or understories. Additionally, I quantified the flocking behavior of the brown-headed nuthatch by measuring its distance to the nearest Carolina chickadee or tufted titmouse at ten-minute intervals. To determine if variations in flocking were driven by interspecific attraction rather than resource distribution or bird density, I employed a simulation approach, randomly distributing fictitious birds onto maps based on observed habitat use and census data. Inter-bird distances were compared across sites using two-tailed Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests.
10.1111_j.0033-0124.1996.00284.x: We examine the electoral geography of the 1994 referenda on European Union membership in Norway, Sweden, and Finland to understand the spatial distribution of support and the factors mediating voter behavior. Utilizing a contextual approach that moves beyond neoclassical economic expectancies, we analyze voting patterns at the national and subnational levels, specifically focusing on the relationship between support for membership and population density across Nordic provinces and counties. We employ indicator-variable regression equations to quantify the correlation between population density and the strength of the "yes" vote, while mapping the geographic concentration of "no" votes against areas eligible for Objective 6 EU structural subsidies. By integrating these quantitative spatial analyses with a qualitative review of the political, social, and economic arguments raised during the campaigns—such as sovereignty, market access, and rural depopulation—we evaluate how local and national differences influenced the interpretation of EU membership. This multi-scalar analysis allows us to discern the tension between the urbanized core and the rural periphery, highlighting how geopolitical orientations and historical contexts shaped the final outcomes in each of the three nations.
10.1111_j.0033-0124.1996.00321.x:
To develop a national rural sampling frame for the HIV Cost and Services Utilization Study (HCSUS), we employed the spatial-order method as a computationally efficient alternative to traditional location-allocation models. Our study area encompassed the 48 conterminous United States, focusing specifically on 1,853 rural (non-MSA) counties and health districts. We utilized county-level AIDS caseload data for the period from January 1991 to December 1993, collected directly from state health agencies and processed using ArcInfo GIS software. 

The methodological approach utilized space-filling (Peano) curves to transform the two-dimensional x,y coordinates of county centroids into a single linear spatial-order value between 0 and 1. By treating the spatial order as a proxy for nearness, we grouped areal units consecutively to create clusters. We established three primary criteria for these clusters: the inclusion of only rural units, a capacity constraint of approximately 50 AIDS cases per cluster to ensure a viable sample size, and the maximization of geographic contiguity. This process resulted in the generation of 226 rural clusters, which served as the primary sampling units for a three-stage probability sampling design. This approach allowed us to manage a large national dataset within a short lead time while maintaining satisfactory spatial clustering.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.1989.tb00273.x: In this presidential address, we review the extensive body of scholarly literature concerning the European colonization of rural North America to evaluate the descriptive and explanatory frameworks employed by historical geographers. We first synthesize seven primary descriptive themes—region, diffusion, landscape, first effective settlement, simplification, environmental perception, and humans as modifiers of the earth—which have traditionally served to document the "what" of Euroamerican settlement. We then critically examine the three dominant causal paradigms used to explain these patterns: environmental determinism, which emphasizes the shaping power of the New World wilderness; economic determinism, which focuses on resource annexation, market access, and capitalistic accumulation; and cultural determinism, which attributes societal evolution to imported ideologies and kinship networks. By analyzing these multi-generational approaches, we argue that while these models provide significant empirical insight, they often lack holistic explanatory power. Consequently, we propose the integration of cultural ecology, specifically the concept of preadaptation, as a potent but underutilized explanatory device to be used in conjunction with economic and cultural determinism to better interpret the processes of overseas farm settlement.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.1992.tb01967.x: We explore the indigenous cultivated landscapes of Mesoamerica as they existed on the eve of the Columbian Encounter, aiming to illustrate the diversity of Amerindian agricultural technologies and management practices. To achieve this, we employ a transect-based approach, examining three specific routes that approximate the initial Spanish *entradas* through the region: the "Cortes transect," extending from the Gulf coast to central Mexico; the "Montejo transect," traversing the Yucatan peninsula from north to south; and the "Alvarado transect," climbing from the Pacific coastal plain into highland Guatemala. By analyzing these diverse environmental and sociopolitical terrains, we identify a multiplicity of cultivation systems, ranging from rainfed *temporal* and shifting cultivation to sophisticated hydraulically transformed wetlands and *chinampas*. We further sketch the major transformations occurring during the first phase of Spanish domination, focusing on the drivers of change—specifically Amerindian depopulation, the introduction of exotic biota and technologies, and the reordering of the rural economy. Through this synthesis, we evaluate the scale of environmental transformation and degradation associated with the transition from indigenous to hybrid landscapes in the sixteenth century.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.1992.tb01969.x:
We examine the processes of Spanish colonization and the subsequent alienation of indigenous landholdings in Central Mexico between 1521 and 1620. Our approach utilizes archival documentation of land grants (*mercedes*) as a primary tool to decipher regional settlement histories and evaluate the efficacy of the Spanish legal system in safeguarding Indian property rights. To explicate the granting of lands, we analyze three specific geographic examples: the Basin of Mexico, the district northwest of Puebla, and the Valley of Toluca. 

We trace the transition from an initial phase of exploitation based on the *encomienda*—a system of tribute and limited labor conscription—to a second phase centered on formal land ownership and agricultural expansion. We specifically analyze the failure of the Puebla experiment (1531–34), which attempted to establish a class of Spanish smallholders, and contrast it with the successful consolidation of large estates by the colonial elite. By synthesizing historical records with spatial considerations, we examine how the Indian demographic collapse and the policy of settlement amalgamation (*congregacion*) facilitated the transfer of land. Through this analysis, we identify structural similarities in the heart of Central Mexico while noting the unique settlement histories of peripheral areas.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.1992.tb01974.x:
This essay employs a historical and biographical analysis to examine the evolution of physical and cultural geography resulting from the Spanish encounter with the Americas between 1492 and 1590. The author challenges the notion of intellectual stasis during the Renaissance by demonstrating how the confrontation with the New World necessitated a shift from Medieval deductivism and Classical ontogenies toward empirical, inductive methods of observation and synthesis. To illustrate this transition, the study focuses on seven exemplary individuals—including Christopher Columbus, Fernando Colón, and Acosta—whose diverse backgrounds as sailors, soldiers, and scholars represent the multilinear development of the period's geographical thought. 

The methodological approach involves a critical re-appraisal of primary documentation, including voyage diaries, letters, and the extensive "Itinerary" survey conducted by Fernando Colón in Spain. By analyzing these sources, the author identifies key methodological spheres such as landscape and biotic taxonomy, cultural recording, and regional synthesis. The analysis specifically tracks the emergence of geomorphological and biogeographical observations and the role of normative urban planning in the Spanish government. Through these case studies, the paper argues that the practical necessity of describing unfamiliar environments fostered a spontaneous capacity for empirical description that often superseded traditional academic curricula.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.1993.tb01924.x: This study adopts the perspective of cultural geography to examine the residential squares of London as symbolic landscapes that express shifting social values during the transition from late feudal to early capitalist modes of production. By analyzing the transformation of urban morphology from the seventeenth to the mid-nineteenth century, we seek to explain the relationship between social processes and landscape patterns, specifically focusing on the use of ornamental vegetation and the role of nature in the human environment. Our approach integrates architectural and landscape aesthetics with an analysis of the ongoing struggle between private and public control over urban open spaces. We examine the evolution of these squares—from paved, public plazas based on continental models to enclosed, private parks reminiscent of rural landscape parks—as expressions of elite tastes and a growing desire for class segregation and domestic isolation. By situating these gardens within the broader context of aristocratic estate development and the extinguishment of collective rights, we illustrate how these urban forms reflected changing concepts of land as capital and property, ultimately influencing the development of early public parks and the value systems underlying modern suburban living.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.1994.tb01727.x:
To examine the relationship between groundwater exploitation and community sustainability, we conducted a multi-scalar analysis of 32 western Kansas counties within the Ogallala region. We measured the association between reported groundwater use in 1990 and population change between 1980 and 1990, utilizing three distinct spatial scales: the county, the rural census subdivision, and the census-designated place. To improve upon previous studies that relied on irrigated acreage—which we argue masks actual water dependence—we employed reported water-use data from the Division of Water Resources of the Kansas State Board of Agriculture as our primary indicator of irrigation intensity. 

We performed multiple regression analyses to determine the relative importance of various independent variables, including the size of the largest place in 1980, per capita income, farm employment percentages, and changes in farm size and irrigated acres. By examining standardized regression coefficients (Beta weights), we sought to isolate the impact of groundwater use per square mile on population fluctuations while controlling for other socioeconomic factors. This methodological approach allows us to determine whether population growth is a function of general county-level location or is specifically contingent upon proximity to intensively irrigated areas and the achievement of certain population thresholds.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.1995.tb01793.x:
We examined the interrelationships between meteorological conditions and landform controls on an estuarine sand beach located 15 km north of the mouth of Delaware Bay at Green Creek, New Jersey. To assess beach response during the post-storm recovery phase, we monitored wind, wave, and current dynamics across three storm sequences in October. We deployed a triangular array of pressure transducers to determine wave approach direction and significant wave height, alongside two Marsh McBirney electromagnetic current meters to measure cross-shore and longshore flow velocities. Wind speed and direction were recorded via an anemometer and wind vane mounted 6.0 m above the ground. 

Wave and flow data were collected at semidiurnal intervals during high tide, with frequency increasing to hourly during storm events. We utilized the BMDP software program for univariate and bivariate spectral analysis to define wave period and angle. Morphological changes were monitored using standard beach surveying techniques, including transit, stadia rod, and depth-of-activity rods across three shore-normal profile lines. Sediment characteristics were analyzed from surface samples using graphic measures. By controlling for wind speed, water depth, and wave height, we compared the effects of different wind directions—specifically north, west, and northeast—on nearshore processes and profile recovery.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.1995.tb01808.x: We examine the ecological and economic impacts of land stabilization techniques on loessal soils in Zhongyang County, Shanxi Province, PRC. Our research focuses on comparing the productivity of two primary stabilization methods against two types of unstabilized land: terraces constructed by human labor (*rengong titian*), terraces constructed using earth-moving equipment (*jixie titian*), flat unterraced land (*yuandi*), and unimproved slopeland (*podian*), the latter serving as a control. We conducted a field survey of 330 households across 33 villages between October 1991 and August 1992. To ensure representative data, we randomly selected four of the county's eleven townships and employed a nested random sample of approximately 84 households per township. We evaluated six primary subsistence and economic crops—corn, millet, soybeans, sunflower, white potatoes, and sorghum—which account for over 92 percent of the land sown by our sample. To analyze the data, we generated production functions for each crop to determine the specific contribution of the four land types to gross production per plot. Additionally, we utilized multiple analysis of variance (MANOVA) to determine if mean yields for these six crops differed significantly across the four land types.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.1996.tb01761.x: To document the rural landscape and structural manifestations of reprivatization in eastern Germany following the 1990 reunification, I employ a multi-methodological approach combining quantitative regional data with qualitative site-specific analysis. I utilize three primary data sources: questionnaires collected by a German agricultural research institute from a representative sample of 728 farms across eastern Germany, topographic and cadastral maps, and extensive field work conducted at nine farm or village sites. Through map analysis and field observations, I document changes in field patterns—specifically the number, density, and shape of fields—before and after reunification at six primary sites, including Heinersdorf, Rehmsdorf, Dedelow, and Hasenholz. To provide a comparative spatial context, I select two additional sites—Uelzen in western Germany and Emerald, Wisconsin—to contrast eastern German field patterns since 1990. The questionnaire data serve to provide the broader regional context for the farm and field changes observed in these case studies. This empirical framework allows me to relate recent landscape transformations to the preceding political and economic shifts of the past fifty years, specifically the Soviet land reforms (1945–1949) and the GDR’s subsequent collectivization (1950–1990).
10.1111_j.1467-8306.2004.00418.x:
To operationalize recent calls for a more actor-oriented and ethnographically informed understanding of rural livelihoods, I reconstruct the dynamics of the regional dugout canoe trade in Central America's Mosquitia from the early 1600s to the late 1990s. My methodological approach combines diachronic historical analysis with contemporary ethnographic fieldwork conducted between 1994 and 2002, specifically focusing on the Patuca River basin in Honduras. To reconstruct the first three centuries of the trade, I draw upon a diverse array of primary archival sources, including 17th-century buccaneers' accounts and 18th- and 19th-century narratives from travelers and merchants. For the 20th century, I integrate missionary reports with testimonies recorded from canoe producers during my fieldwork. 

Drawing on the "social life of things" and theories of access within commodity chains, I treat the dugout canoe as a constant object of analysis to expose the multiscaled social and political systems through which it circulates. By analyzing the "biography" of this commodity, I examine key variables such as ethnic production specialization, the interaction between local and international trade circuits, and the constraints facing undercapitalized households. This trade-network approach allows me to move beyond binary calibrations of rural life—such as "autarkic" versus "market-integrated"—to reveal how remote rural peoples negotiate and resist asymmetrical relationships with global capital.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.2005.00450.x:
To explore the complexities of a coupled human-environment system, we developed the Integrative Model for Simulating Household and Ecosystem Dynamics (IMSHED), an agent-based spatial model implemented using the Swarm toolkit. Our study focuses on the Wolong Nature Reserve in China, where we simulate the impact of a growing rural population on giant panda habitat. We employ a bottom-up approach, treating households as the fundamental decision-making units. These household agents are equipped with "knowledge" of themselves and their environment, interacting via artificial-intelligence rules to simulate the coevolution of human demographics and forest dynamics. 

The methodology integrates multidisciplinary data sources, including remote-sensing imagery for spatial heterogeneity and extensive socioeconomic data from in-person interviews with 220 households conducted between 1998 and 2002. The model incorporates three primary components: a demographic submodel tracking individual life histories and household formation (informed by structural equation modeling), a fuelwood demand module based on household size, age composition, and cropland area, and a spatial module simulating forest growth and harvesting. By linking individual-level behaviors to macroscopic landscape changes, we capture nonlinear interactions, feedbacks, and time lags across varying spatial and temporal scales to provide a tool for biodiversity conservation and policy analysis.
10.1111_j.1467-8306.2005.00466.x: Drawing upon postcolonial studies and postcolonial geographies of North America, we examine the production of southern difference in the postbellum U.S. South between 1865 and 1880. We analyze a series of travel accounts penned by northern men and women to investigate how the region was scripted as an "imperial holding" of the North/nation, effectively translating a regional binary into a colonizer-colonized distinction. Our methodological approach focuses on the spatialities of description and the mechanisms of knowledge production within these narratives. Specifically, we identify and analyze three recurring textual themes—discourses of civilization, descriptions of nature, and discussions of whiteness—to illustrate how northern travelers positioned the South as a colonial appendage. We further interrogate how gender contoured the "civilizing mission" directed toward emancipated African Americans and the role of the imperial explorer in rural sites. By treating the South as a process rather than a fixed place, we employ a postcolonial lens to highlight the contradictions and imperfections of this internal imperialism, particularly where white rural poverty problematized the separation between the northern colonizer and the southern colonized.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2003.tb00035.x:
We conducted two in situ surveys in December 1999 to analyze local dependence on forest resources within the Shimentai Nature Reserve (SNR) in Yingde County, Guangdong Province, China. Utilizing a stratified sampling approach, we selected seven village committees for study, categorized into "near-zone" groups (those within or possessing rights to the reserve) and "far-zone" groups (those located in the Lianjiang River floodplain). Our data collection integrated four primary methods: questionnaire surveys, face-to-face interviews, organized group discussions, and in situ observations, supplemented by a review of local gazetteers and official reserve records. 

The primary questionnaire survey was administered to 297 randomly selected households (*jia*), focusing on forest product collection, income and expenditure profiles, and socioeconomic variables including ethnicity (Hakka and Yao), household size, wealth, and out-migration. To further probe historical and current use patterns, we conducted in-depth supplementary surveys with 29 high-dependence households. To minimize non-sampling errors and overcome linguistic barriers, we recruited and trained local primary school teachers as research assistants. Data were analyzed using SPSS 9.0, employing Student's t-tests to gauge distance factors, Mann-Whitney tests for ethnic variables, and one-way analysis of variance to evaluate the influence of wealth, household size, and migrant labor on resource utilization.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2003.tb00036.x: I examine the formation of social capital—defined as the norms of trust and reciprocity integral to social relations—and the ways in which it enables rural peoples' organizations to access rights and resources. I argue that this social capital is embedded within and given meaning through a constellation of shared understandings, or cultural capital. To illustrate this framework, I conduct a detailed case study of the Federation of Indigenous Organizations of Napo (FOIN), a lowland Quichua organization in the Ecuadorian Amazon. My approach probes the organizational history of FOIN, emphasizing the institutional relationships it has forged over time across multiple scales, from local community levels to provincial, regional, and transnational networks. I analyze how these connections have facilitated the federation's ability to manage development projects and make political claims against the state. Additionally, I employ a survey of seventy-four Quichua visitors to FOIN's offices in Tena to investigate the uneven distribution of social capital, specifically examining how variables such as gender, linguistic ability (Spanish vs. Quichua), and spatial proximity to the federation's headquarters influence access to organizational benefits. Through this analysis, I explore the role of FOIN in constructing the symbolic meanings within which its social relations and material practices are understood.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2004.tb00178.x:
Drawing upon a feminist political ecology framework, we conducted a qualitative study to explore the role of women in nature-society relations through the lens of "kitchenspace"—the integrated indoor and outdoor areas of the house-lot garden used for food preparation. Our research took place over eleven months between 2000 and 2001 across three semiurban communities in central Mexico: Xochimilco, Ocotepec, and Tetecala. These sites represent a gradient of urbanization and cultural identity, ranging from the periurban suburbs of Mexico City and Cuernavaca to the more isolated regional center of Tetecala. 

To capture the embodied experience of "dwelling" in these spaces, we employed a tripartite methodological approach consisting of participant observation, ethnographic interviews, and participatory mapping. We spent extensive time in approximately half a dozen homes per site, engaging in the daily labor of food preparation and the collective staging of religious fiestas. After an initial period of unstructured interviews and observation, we implemented structured interviews focusing on three key variables: gendered landscape spaces and adaptation to change, women's ecological knowledge regarding food gathering, and narratives of cultural identity. This approach allowed us to document the transmission of gendered knowledge and the symbolic connection to the land within increasingly urbanized contexts.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2004.tb00182.x: We conducted this study to examine how improvements in transportation infrastructure have influenced economic specialization within the woodworking industry of the Meseta Purepecha in west-central Michoacan, Mexico. We focused our analysis on seven towns, selected for their representation of varying levels of accessibility and their reliance on woodworking as a dominant craft industry. Our methodological approach combined longitudinal data with primary field research conducted in 2001 and 2004. To assess changes in production and market reach, we interviewed fifty-four craft producers (approximately eight per town), gathering data on production methods, raw material sources, and market destinations. To analyze the spatial distribution of the industry, we performed comprehensive street surveys in each of the seven towns during peak business hours (10:00 A.M. to 2:00 P.M.) in both 2001 and 2004 to map the location of craft storefronts. These current observations were then compared against historical data from the 1940s and 1950s to evaluate shifts in the types of crafts produced and the evolution of local and external market linkages relative to the development of the regional road network.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2010.00019.x: We examine the synanthropic relationship between the American crow (*Corvus brachyrhynchos*) and human populations in eastern North America through a multiscalar geographical lens. By integrating time scales ranging from the prehistoric to the observable present, we analyze how anthropogenic landscape modifications—specifically the fragmentation of closed forests—have driven the species' biogeography. Our approach utilizes a combination of historical observations, ornithological records, and archaeological evidence to correlate crow distributions with human land-use patterns. At the regional scale, we track the expansion of the species alongside the diffusion of maize (*Zea mays*) from the late Archaic period through Euro-American settlement, contrasting the crow's presence in humanized landscapes with the prevalence of ravens (*Corvus corax*) in wilderness areas. At the microscale, we analyze the foraging efficiency of crows within the agricultural cycle of the cornfield. Finally, we document the diverse behavioral and technological strategies employed by Native American peoples and Euro-American settlers to mitigate crop depredation, treating the crow as a biological indicator of the "pristine myth" and a reflection of the evolving human-environment interface.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2010.00041.x: To assess the merit of unifying the concepts of creative destruction and trial by space, we conducted a detailed study of Creemore, a historic village in Ontario, Canada. Our methodological approach sought to determine how the village evolved from a productivist townscape into its current form as a postproductivist heritagescape. To capture the essence of Creemore as a locality and evaluate its evolutionary path, we drew upon a diverse array of data sources. We distributed a questionnaire to 300 households during the autumn of 2007 to elicit residents' opinions on the village's development, achieving a response rate of 25 percent. This primary data was supplemented by a comprehensive content analysis of print and online materials, including historical and contemporary municipal planning documents, reports and brochures from the Creemore Business Improvement Association, and articles from the local weekly newspaper, the *Creemore Star/Echo*, published since 1981. Additionally, we utilized historical business data from the 1977 Dun and Bradstreet Reference Book to establish a baseline for the village's commercial landscape. Together, these sources allowed us to analyze the interplay between the three facets of rural space—locality, representation, and everyday lives—and the motivational drivers of profit, preservation, and promotion.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2014.12027.x:
We employed a qualitative case-study approach to examine the migration patterns of former smallholders to peri-urban interface (PUI) zones in western Pará state, Brazil, specifically focusing on communities near the city of Santarém. Our methodology integrated a suite of qualitative tools, including ethnographically oriented household surveys, over sixty hours of semistructured interviews, and four weeks of participant observation. We utilized purposive "snowball" sampling to recruit a diverse participant pool that included both *caboclos* and *colonos*, as well as migrants and nonmigrants, smallholder subsistence farmers, and midsized commercial farmers. 

The study was conducted across three specific sites within the municipalities of Santarém and Belterra: the town of Alter do Chão (specifically the neighborhoods of União and Nova União) and the communities of São Jorge and Piquiatuba, the latter two located within the Tapajós National Forest (FLONA). By analyzing these sites, we sought to identify the push and pull factors influencing the decision to resettle in PUI zones rather than returning to rural origins or remaining in urban centers. Key variables under consideration included access to infrastructure (education and healthcare), the availability of wage labor, the ability to maintain subsistence activities, and the impact of federal conditional cash-transfer programs on livelihood stability.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2014.12037.x: We employ a primarily descriptive research design to investigate the role of urban-rural remittances in fostering self-reliance and development within Shubbola, a rural community in northern Kordofan State, western Sudan. Our methodology integrates qualitative and quantitative approaches, utilizing in-depth interviews, focus-group discussions, and direct observation, supplemented by a review of secondary data. Fieldwork was conducted between January 2008 and January 2011. We utilized a systematic sampling technique along a central street transect to select seventy male heads of households for semi-structured interviews, focusing on variables such as remittance receipt, crop types, farm size fluctuations over the previous decade, and the adoption of tractor technology. To contextualize these findings and assess Shubbola's influence on its hinterland, we conducted two focus-group discussions: one with ten farmers from adjacent villages at the weekly market and another with local youth at the village evening club. Data analysis involved basic descriptive statistics—specifically frequency distributions and averages—processed via IBM SPSS, while qualitative data from key informants and observations were used to establish associations between remittance flows and socioeconomic transformations.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2014.12044.x:
To examine how residents of the rural Mexican municipio of Coxcatlán, Puebla, construct garbage as a set of place-specific and demographically contingent issues, I employed a modified form of participatory risk mapping. Conducted between August and November 2011, the study sampled 433 respondents across twelve communities, targeting heads of households or the adults primarily responsible for waste management. I utilized a two-stage ordinal ranking system: respondents first identified the most important garbage-related issues—using open-ended prompts such as *asunto* (issue) or *lo que nos afecta* (that which affects us)—and subsequently rank-ordered these issues by importance. 

To analyze the data, I calculated an incidence score (the proportion of the sample mentioning a specific issue) and an importance index (a normalized rank derived from the number of issues listed per respondent). These values were plotted on two-dimensional coordinate grids to visualize the prevalence and severity of various issues. To further explore the nuances of these perceptions, I stratified the population by sex and spatial location (valley vs. sierra, municipal seat vs. outlying settlements, and center vs. peripheral colonias). Finally, I applied Pearson's Chi-Square statistics and binary logistic regression to test for statistically significant relationships between these spatial and demographic variables and the incidence of the most frequently cited garbage issues.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2015.12078.x:
We utilize data from the National Research on Education in Agrarian Reform (*Pesquisa Nacional de Educação na Reforma Agrária* - PNERA), the only available settlement survey of its kind, to explore the success of land-reform settlements in improving living standards and providing educational access for the rural poor. Conducted in 2004 by the Ministry of Education in collaboration with INCRA and INEP, the PNERA dataset provides a comprehensive snapshot of federal land-reform settlements legalized since 1985. Our methodological approach involves a comparative analysis between the Landless Rural Workers Movement (*Movimento dos Trabalhadores Rurais Sem-Terra* - MST) and other smaller, more fragmented settlement movements. 

The PNERA data is derived from three distinct survey groups: school representatives (teachers and principals) from 8,679 schools; community leaders from 5,595 settlements; and a representative sample of 10,220 households across 510 randomly selected settlements per state. By applying sample weights, we analyze key variables including racial distribution, age, gender, and self-perceived social status, alongside indicators of residential environment and material possessions. This allows us to assess the internal mechanisms of the MST—specifically its bottom-up organizational structure and commitment to participatory education—relative to the broader landscape of Brazilian agrarian reform.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2016.12161.x:
We conducted a case study of agricultural tourism in Michigan to evaluate the sector's economic contributions to the state, business operators, and employees. Our research design employed a mixed-methods approach, beginning with qualitative focus groups and depth interviews with specialty crop operators to inform the development of a systematic survey. We identified a statewide mailing list of 2,974 operations using Chamber of Commerce websites, directories, and online guides. The survey, administered via mail and Qualtrics during the summer and fall of 2013, collected data on firm demographics, product inventories, employment, advertising costs, and perceived economic benefits from 154 usable responses.

To assess the broader economic impact, we calculated descriptive statistics for twelve categories of operations and extrapolated these findings to a conservative statewide estimate of 4,000 operations. Furthermore, we utilized Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) multivariate regression analysis to identify drivers of firm success. The dependent variable was standardized as gross receipts per day of operation to allow for comparison across seasonal and year-round businesses. Independent variables included mean daily advertising costs, annual wages, the number of days open to the public, the percentage of sales from off-farm products, and a dummy variable to account for scale effects based on an annual sales threshold of $20,000.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2016.12182.x: Informed by feminist geopolitics, we employ a microscale, place-specific approach to examine the hidden, embodied costs of neoliberal restructuring and transnational migration borne by those "left behind." Our methodology integrates quantitative and qualitative data to disentangle how global political processes are experienced through localized, everyday practices. We draw upon socioeconomic data from a Mexican Migration Project (MMP) ethnosurvey of 150 randomly sampled households in Tierra Azul, a principal migration center in north-central Veracruz, to frame our analysis of gendered impacts. To supplement this, we conducted 63 semi-structured, in-depth interviews between 2007 and 2009 with 66 individuals—including community leaders, migrants, and family members—across 17 different communities in the Totonac region of rural Veracruz. Using purposeful and snowball sampling, we targeted information-rich informants, particularly nonmigrant women, children, and the elderly, to capture the narratives of de facto female-headed households. By contrasting female- and male-headed households and analyzing lived experiences of social reproduction and care work, we seek to challenge macroscale "migration for development" discourses that prioritize economic indicators over the psychosocial and corporal costs of family separation.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2016.12200.x: This study explores the everyday lived experiences of the American exclave of Point Roberts, Washington, by examining its historical geography, evolving economic functions, and the social dynamics of its residential population. To capture the complexities of this hybrid borderland, I employed a mixed-methods approach combining quantitative analysis of public data sets with qualitative field research. Quantitative data were drawn from the American Community Survey, United States and Canadian censuses, Department of Homeland Security border crossing records, and the 2014 Whatcom County property tax parcel geodatabase. These were supplemented by close personal observations dating back to 1991, including a period of immersive participant observation while living in the community during the 2011–2012 academic year. To further develop a comprehensive picture of service provision and the perceptions of life in an exclave, I conducted semistructured interviews between 2011 and 2015 with key informants—including local business owners and Whatcom County planning and utility personnel—as well as members from ten U.S.-citizen households residing in Point Roberts on a year-round or part-time basis.
10.1111_j.1931-0846.2016.12206.x:
We employ a dynamic time series analysis using a household panel dataset collected across four survey waves (2007, 2008, 2010, and 2013) in three rural Vietnamese provinces: Ha Tinh, Thua Thien-Hue, and Dak Lak. These study areas were selected to represent diverse regional economic structures, ranging from the cash-crop specialization of the central highlands to the more diversified, industrialized economies of the north-central coast. To operationalize vulnerability to poverty, we utilize the absolute poverty line to generate binary dependent variables: the probability of remaining in poverty for previously poor households and the probability of falling into poverty for non-poor households. 

Our empirical framework utilizes region-specific logistic panel regressions to analyze the influence of becoming self-employed on these probabilities. We distinguish between opportunity-driven and necessity-driven self-employment; since motivational data were only available in the final wave, we approximate opportunity-driven self-employment using instrumental variables—specifically, the scale of initial investment and the number of hired non-family employees. We control for external shocks (categorized as individual, natural, and economic) and household asset endowments, including human, social, natural, and financial capital. This approach allows us to evaluate how the interaction between household motivations and regional economic environments—such as infrastructure quality and market proximity—shapes the likelihood of escaping poverty.
10.2307_215196: This study examines the historical and spatial distribution of the black population in southeastern Florida, focusing on the eastern portions of Palm Beach, Broward, and Dade counties. I trace the settlement patterns of blacks and their relationship to white populations from the post-Reconstruction era through the post-civil-rights period to explain the evolution of racial locations. Utilizing 1990 U.S. Census data, I analyze the distribution of the population across 630 census tracts, omitting twelve outlying tracts in Palm Beach County to focus on the regional ecumene. To test the accuracy of "belt" or "corridor" descriptions of black residential areas, I employ a zonal and transect analysis in Broward and Palm Beach counties, aggregating 364 census tracts into five north-south zones and twenty east-west transects. This methodology allows for the identification of population maxima and minima across a grid of one hundred cells. Finally, I apply the classical central-place model as an analytical device to demonstrate how racial bias and restricted mobility created separate sets of central-place hexagons for black residents, resulting in fragmented, disconnected communities separated by commercial districts or white residential zones.
10.2307_215199:
This study examines the environmental and political history of the prickly pear cactus infestation in eastern Australia from 1880 to 1940. Utilizing a historical-geographical approach, I analyze the diffusion of the pest across Queensland and New South Wales, focusing on the interaction between climatic and edaphic characteristics—specifically subhumid regions with bentonitic soils—and human-mediated dispersal. The methodology relies on the synthesis of contemporary survey data, royal commission reports, and historical estimates to map the exponential growth and eventual collapse of the infestation, which at its peak rendered approximately 250,000 square kilometers of rural land useless. 

I evaluate the efficacy of governmental responses by categorizing interventions into indirect policies, such as land-tenure legislation and coercive leasehold regulations, and direct policies, including mechanical clearance, chemical poisoning, and the extermination of seed-dispersing native fauna. By tracing a pattern of "reactive escalation," I argue that early state-government strategies were uncoordinated and haphazard, often unwittingly promoting the spread of the pest. The analysis concludes by assessing the transition toward biological control, specifically the introduction of *Cactoblastis cactorum*, weighing the role of cohesive policy against opportunistic discovery in the eventual eradication of the menace.
10.2307_215251: To provide a model of urban tourism for small Caribbean islands—defined as those with populations under 400,000—I draw upon empirical evidence from eight principal port cities: St. John's (Antigua), Nassau (Bahamas), Bridgetown (Barbados), Roseau (Dominica), Castries (St. Lucia), Plymouth (Montserrat), St. George's (Grenada), and Kingstown (St. Vincent). My approach involves analyzing the spatial evolution of these centers from a "pretourism" phase, characterized by a lack of autonomous tourism structures, to a mature phase of development. By examining the intersection of cruise-ship activity, stayover tourism, and urban morphology, I develop a zonation model that depicts the geographical distribution of tourist activity. This model identifies five distinct zones: a specialized tourist zone adjacent to the docks, the central business district (further divided into transitional and peripheral areas), local neighborhoods, the resort strip, and the rural periphery. The analysis focuses on variables such as distance-decay patterns of tourist movement, the role of physical barriers, and the functional differentiation between retail-focused excursionist zones and accommodation-focused resort strips to generalize the patterns of urban tourism space across the region.
10.2307_215274: To examine geographical variations in fertility rates across Africa and identify the underlying influences of the fertility transition, I employ a spatial-temporal framework based on demographic transition theory. Using country-level data from 1980 and 1993, I conduct a multivariate analysis to assess spatial variations and changes in fertility. The study area is divided into five regions—north, west, east, middle, and south—to account for differing sociocultural and economic characteristics. I utilize six measures of fertility as dependent variables, including crude birthrates and total fertility rates, and examine fourteen independent variables categorized as socioeconomic (e.g., GNP, primary-school enrollment, urban population percentage, and the Human Development Index for females) and demographic (e.g., infant mortality rates and contraceptive prevalence). To determine the levels of regional variation and account for observed spatial patterns, I employ a suite of quantitative techniques, including pairwise t-tests for means, analysis of variance (ANOVA), correlation analysis, and stepwise multiple-regression. These methods are used to operationalize a causal model where socioeconomic development is hypothesized as the primary driver of fertility decline.
10.2307_215379: This study examines the relationship between global market participation and household vulnerability to natural hazards in rural Western Samoa following Hurricane Ofa in February 1990. Utilizing a case study approach, I conducted fieldwork from April to August 1990 in two villages on the island of Savaii: Fusi, which had a longer history of market integration and higher rates of wage labor, and Vaipua, which was less integrated. To assess vulnerability and the persistence of the "moral economy," I employed a stratified sampling method, selecting fifteen households per village across high, average, and low socioeconomic categories. Data collection involved household interviews, land-use surveys, and general observations. Specifically, I conducted dietary surveys in May to track staple food consumption and a subsequent survey in June to quantify nonmonetary food gifts given and received. Additionally, a village-wide census from May through July was used to evaluate hurricane damage and the pace of repairs. By analyzing variables such as crop specialization (specifically taro versus coconut), the frequency of reciprocal food exchanges, and the reliance on imported staples, I sought to determine whether incorporation into the global economy had eroded traditional social coping mechanisms and increased the vulnerability of marginal households.
10.2307_215825:
This study analyzes the territorial dimensions of the Shining Path insurgency in Peru from its inception in 1980 through the early 1990s. I employ a conceptual framework based on a three-stage military-political process—mobile warfare, guerrilla warfare, and the establishment of a territorial-based insurgent state—to evaluate the movement's progress toward statehood. The analysis focuses on the spatial evolution of the conflict, tracing the insurgency's origins in the southern Andean departments of Ayacucho and Apurimac and its subsequent expansion into the central Andes, the tropical rain forests of the upper Huallaga Valley, the southern altiplano of Puno, and the urban environment of Lima.
